title: Articulation in Motion: Prior-free Part Mobility Analysis for Articulated Objects By Dynamic-Static Disentanglement
name: aim-prior-free-articulation
short_hypothesis: By analyzing an object interaction video instead of two static states, a proposed dual-Gaussian representation can disentangle static and dynamic components. This clean separation of motion cues will enable a subsequent sequential RANSAC algorithm to perform robust, prior-free part segmentation and articulation analysis, automatically determining the number of parts and their kinematics.
related_work: Recent methods for articulated object analysis typically rely on observing the object in two distinct states (start and end). These approaches perform per-point segmentation and optimize articulation, but often require a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Their performance degrades when there is no clear geometric correspondence between the two states, such as when new parts are revealed during motion (e.g., the interior of a refrigerator). This reliance on two static states and prior knowledge about the part count limits their application and robustness.
abstract: Articulated objects are ubiquitous in daily life. Our goal is to achieve a high-quality reconstruction, segmentation of independent moving parts, and analysis of articulation. Recent methods analyse two different articulation states and perform per-point part segmentation, optimising per-part articulation using cross-state correspondences, given a priori knowledge of the number of parts. Such assumptions greatly limit their applications and performance. Their robustness is reduced when objects cannot be clearly visible in both states. To address these issues, in this paper, we present a new framework, Articulation in Motion (AiM). We infer part-level decomposition, articulation kinematics, and reconstruct an interactive 3D digital replica from a user–object interaction video and a start-state scan. We propose a dual-Gaussian scene representation that is learned from an initial 3DGS scan of the object and a video that shows the movement of separate parts. It uses motion cues to segment the object into parts and assign articulation joints. Subsequently, a robust, sequential RANSAC is employed to achieve part mobility analysis without any part-level structural priors, which clusters moving primitives into rigid parts and estimates kinematics while automatically determining the number of parts. The proposed approach separates the object into parts, each represented as a 3D Gaussian set, enabling high-quality rendering.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method (AIM) on part segmentation, reconstruction quality, and articulation estimation accuracy against state-of-the-art two-state methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a synthetic dataset rendered from PartNet-Mobility objects, including challenging two-part, three-part, and multi-part objects where interior parts are gradually revealed. AIM will be given a multi-view scan of the start state and an interaction video (200-500 frames). Baselines will be given 100 random views of the start and end states. The method will be compared against baselines: PARIS†, DTA, and ArtGS. A baseline version of the proposed method, Ours-b, which uses a single deformable 3DGS, will also be evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Part segmentation performance using 3D Intersection-over-Union (IoU).', 'Reconstruction quality using bi-directional Chamfer Distance (mm).', 'Articulation estimation accuracy using Axis Angle Error (°), Axis Position Error (0.1m), and Part Motion (° or m).']

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of individual components of the AIM framework through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on complex objects from the synthetic dataset (Storage-47648, Table-31249). The full model will be compared against several ablated versions: one without the start state scan, one without the static-during-motion detection (SDMD) module, one without the dual-Gaussian representation (using a single deformable 3DGS), and one without sequential RANSAC (attempting DB-SCAN or K-means instead).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average Axis Angle Error (Ang_avg)', 'Average Axis Pose Error (Pose_avg)', 'Average Part Motion Error (Motion_ang/avg)', 'Average Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m_avg)', 'Average 3D IoU on dynamic parts (3D IoU_d_avg)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's performance on real-world data captured from a first-person perspective.
experiments.Method: Data will be captured using Meta Project Aria Glasses, recording a user interacting with an oven and a storage cabinet. The video will be processed using COLMAP to estimate camera poses. The performance of AIM will be qualitatively assessed on these sequences.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of rendered views against ground truth.', 'Visual inspection of rendered part masks from the dual-Gaussian representation.', 'Qualitative assessment of the estimated articulation parameters.']

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed method against a pre-trained segmentation-driven approach to demonstrate its effectiveness beyond the self-contained setting.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will use an overlapping subset of objects from the official Video2Articulation dataset, including four two-part objects and one three-part object. The proposed method will be compared against the Video2Articulation baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Axis Angle Error (°)', 'Axis Position Error (0.1m)', 'Chamfer Distance on moving parts (CD-m)', 'Chamfer Distance on static parts (CD-s)']

risk_factors: We do not require the use of depth sensing, as done by most prior art techniques; however, we do utilise a video that captures the DoFs of the object's parts.
Capturing the whole geometry of internal parts, such as drawers, an extended blade of a knife, or the blades of a pair of scissors, requires the full disassembly of the articulated object.
The generated geometry is limited to the visible geometry and, as such, may be limited in its application for developing interactive models.

From the real-world data, we observe that when motion introduces structural ambiguities—particularly those caused by specular reflections, such as the glass door of the oven in the video—our vanilla 3DGS-based reconstruction in AIM can be affected.

title: Knowledge Fusion of Large Language Models
name: LLM-Knowledge-Fusion-FuseLLM
short_hypothesis: It is possible to combine the capabilities of multiple, existing, architecturally-diverse Large Language Models (LLMs) into a single, more potent target LLM. This can be achieved by externalizing the collective knowledge and unique strengths of the source LLMs via their generative probability distributions, and then transferring this fused knowledge to the target LLM through lightweight continual training.
related_work: Prior work on integrating capabilities from multiple models includes ensemble methods and weight merging. Ensemble methods are impractical for LLMs due to high memory and inference time requirements, and they do not facilitate fine-tuning into a single model. Weight merging requires the models to have uniform network architectures, which is a significant limitation. Knowledge distillation is also related, but typically aims for model compression where the student model's performance lags behind the teacher. This work proposes a method to fuse knowledge from architecturally diverse LLMs into a single model that is anticipated to surpass the performance of any individual source model.
abstract: While training large language models (LLMs) from scratch can generate models with distinct functionalities and strengths, it comes at significant costs and may result in redundant capabilities. Alternatively, a cost-effective and compelling approach is to merge existing pre-trained LLMs into a more potent model. However, due to the varying architectures of these LLMs, directly blending their weights is impractical. In this paper, we introduce the notion of knowledge fusion for LLMs, aimed at combining the capabilities of existing LLMs and transferring them into a single LLM. By leveraging the generative distributions of source LLMs, we externalize their collective knowledge and unique strengths, thereby potentially elevating the capabilities of the target model beyond those of any individual source LLM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the general reasoning ability of the fused LLM compared to its source models.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Big-Bench Hard (BBH) benchmark, which contains 23 multiple-choice and 4 free-form generation tasks. Predictions will be generated using few-shot chain-of-thought (CoT) prompts. The source models for fusion are Llama-2 7B, OpenLLaMA 7B, and MPT 7B. The target model is another Llama-2 7B. Baselines for comparison will be the original source LLMs and a Llama-2 7B model continually trained on the MiniPile dataset using only the standard causal language modeling objective (Llama-2 CLM).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the commonsense capability of the fused LLM.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Common Sense (CS) benchmark, comprising 5 multiple-choice tasks: ARC easy and challenge, BoolQ, HellaSwag, and OpenBookQA. A likelihood-based zero-shot evaluation will be conducted. The source, target, and baseline models are the same as in the reasoning experiment (Llama-2 7B, OpenLLaMA 7B, MPT 7B as sources; Llama-2 7B as target; original LLMs and Llama-2 CLM as baselines).

experiments.Description: To assess the coding ability of the fused LLM across multiple programming languages.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on the MultiPL-E benchmark, specifically using zero-shot code generation for 10 programming languages in the HumanEval category. The source, target, and baseline models are the same as in the previous experiments.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of FUSELLM against traditional knowledge distillation.
experiments.Method: FUSELLM will be used to integrate three 7B models (Llama-2, OpenLLaMA, MPT). This will be compared against a conventional knowledge distillation setup where a Llama-2 7B model is trained to distill knowledge from a larger Llama-2 13B model. The evaluation is planned across the BBH, CS, and ME benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To compare the efficacy of FUSELLM with model ensemble and weight merging methods on architecturally identical models.
experiments.Method: A scenario will be simulated where three distinct LLMs with identical structures are created by continually training a Pythia 1B model on three different domain-specific corpora (PhilPapers, NIH ExPorter, USPTO Backgrounds). These three models will then be fused using FUSELLM, an ensemble method (weighted average of probabilities), and a weight merging method.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the applicability of FUSELLM for fusing instruction-tuned models.
experiments.Method: Source models will be created by fine-tuning Llama-2, OpenLLaMA, and MPT on instruction datasets (Evol-Instruct, ShareGPT, Open-Platypus). FUSELLM will then be used to fuse their knowledge into a target model (the instruction-tuned Llama-2).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Instruction-following performance on the Vicuna Benchmark, judged by GPT-4.']

risk_factors: The thoughtful selection of the training corpus can be a crucial consideration, particularly with regard to its relevance to downstream tasks.
In scenarios where the capabilities of source LLMs vary significantly, the fusion function appears to be crucial in effectively combining their respective strengths.

FUSELLM occasionally exhibits degraded performance on tasks such as Geometric Shapes and Word Sorting, which could be attributed to two reasons. First, the other source LLMs, apart from Llama-2, perform poorly on these tasks, affecting the fusion results. Second, the relevance between the continual training dataset and downstream tasks also contributes to the performance degradation.
The distortions introduced by the straightforward weighted summation used in AvgCE, which may diminish the distinct advantages of individual LLMs.

title: Latent Diffusion with LLMs for Reasoning
name: latent-diffusion-for-reasoning
short_hypothesis: Combining latent diffusion models (LDMs) with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address the fundamental shortcomings of autoregressive models for complex reasoning. This is achieved by performing iterative reasoning in a semantically rich latent space, which is not handicapped by the sequential order of tokens and allows for non-sequential, self-correcting reasoning steps.
related_work: Autoregressive large language models (LLMs) are the de-facto standard for natural language generation but struggle with complex reasoning. Their limitations stem from their autoregressive nature, where compute is constant per token and generation is strictly sequential, preventing them from solving easier subproblems first. Prior approaches like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, longer context, and recurrent models have attempted to address these issues but are not general solutions. Discrete text diffusion models (e.g., Diffusion-of-Thought) have shown promise for self-correction but lack the training efficiency of traditional LLMs. This work proposes to merge the benefits of both worlds by using a latent diffusion model on top of representations from a pretrained encoder-decoder LLM.
abstract: Despite the widespread adoption of large language models with hundreds of billions of parameters, these models still struggle on complex reasoning benchmarks. In this paper, we argue that the autoregressive nature of current language models are not suited for reasoning due to fundamental limitations, and that reasoning requires slow accumulation of knowledge through time. We show that combining latent diffusion models with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture provides a scalable way to address some of the fundamental shortcomings posed by autoregressive models. Diffusion models can arrive at predictions through many forward passes in latent space, and their reasoning is not handicapped by the order of the tokens in the dataset. We propose that latent diffusion language models is a feasible approach towards scalable language models that have general complex reasoning abilities.

experiments.Description: To analyze the performance of the proposed hybrid architecture on downstream arithmetic reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed Latent Diffusion model is to be evaluated on custom-created problem sets for single digit addition (3-5 numbers), double digit addition, and single digit three-operation tasks. For single digit addition, Chain-of-Thought reasoning chains are provided as the target. The performance will be compared against fine-tuned BART and various sizes of GPT-3 (400M, 13B, 175B).

experiments.Description: To study the benefits of the proposed latent diffusion augmented LLM on a mock spatial reasoning problem that requires non-trivial reasoning chains to compute the first token.
experiments.Method: A custom spatial reasoning task is created where the model must perform a series of rotations based on input numbers. The proposed Latent Diffusion model will be tested on this task and its performance will be compared against a fine-tuned BART baseline.

experiments.Description: To ablate different architectural variants, diffusion sequence lengths, and model layers to determine the best architecture for scaling and to study the effect of different input text conditioning methods.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies are planned on the Common Crawl (C4) dataset. These include: 1) Comparing different input text conditioning architectures (In-Context, Cross-Attention, AdaLN-Zero). 2) Varying the number of model layers (6, 12, 24). 3) Varying the diffusion sequence length (16, 32, 64). 4) Comparing the use of different pretrained encoder-decoder backbones (BART-base vs. BART-large).

risk_factors: A notable limitation of diffusion is that it is relatively inefficient to train compared to conventional language models.
One hypothesis is that there is a combinatorial explosion as more tokens are diffused at once, hence the gradients are not as well-behaved (noisy gradient landscapes).
However, due to its inefficiencies and other potential obstacles, it remains uncertain how far we can practically scale such architectures with current hardware and algorithms.

We've found that generation length decreases with longer training times from preliminary experiments which led to uncomparable BertScores since longer sequences have higher BertScores on average and that shorter sequences have lower Perplexity on average.
Since experiments are trained with different hyperparameters for different learned generation lengths, metrics cannot be compared between different experiments.

title: Building Neural Networks that are Robust to Adversarial Examples Using Probabilistic Loss Function
name: probabilistic-loss-for-adversarial-robustness
short_hypothesis: Putting a non-zero probability mass on all classes, with the correct class carrying the highest weight, may reduce over-fitting and render a trained DNN less vulnerable to adversarial attacks by modifying the standard cross-entropy loss function.
related_work: Prior defenses against adversarial examples include adversarial training (computationally expensive), defensive distillation and ensemble methods (overhead of multiple models), and input transformations. This work proposes a method that modifies the loss function directly, aiming to avoid the overhead of training additional models or augmenting the input data.
abstract: Adversarial examples are an Achilles heel of deep neural networks, robbing them of their functional performance in mission-critical applications. This work proposes a novel method of making deep neural networks robust to adversarial examples by modifying the loss function to a soft version of cross-entropy loss for classification problems. For regression problems, the data distribution is examined using Bayesian techniques (Gaussian Mixture Model) and the loss function is modified using a posterior probability distribution. The approach is justified using mathematical derivation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed probabilistic loss function in defending against adversarial attacks on the MNIST image classification task.
experiments.Method: A deep neural network based on the Inception CNN architecture will be trained on the MNIST dataset. One model will be trained using the standard cross-entropy loss function as a baseline. A second model will be trained using the proposed calibrated probabilistic loss function (calibrated via Algorithm 1). Both trained models will be subjected to FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by FGSM and Carlini-Wagner L2 attacks.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the defense's effectiveness on a more complex, large-scale image classification task using the Imagenet dataset.
experiments.Method: A model will be trained on the Imagenet dataset using the proposed probabilistic loss function. Its robustness will be tested against the Carlini-Wagner attack.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy on adversarial examples generated by the Carlini-Wagner attack.']

risk_factors: For regression problems, we do not know the number of clusters. Therefore, this becomes a hyper-parameter.
Investigating the effectiveness of other non-Gaussian priors is an avenue for future research.

title: Counterintuitive RL: The Hidden Value of Acting Bad
name: maxmin-td-learning
short_hypothesis: A theoretically-founded improvement to temporal difference learning can be achieved by taking actions that minimize the state-action value function, especially early in training. This counterintuitive strategy is proposed to increase the information gain from environment interactions by generating experiences with higher temporal difference errors, thereby accelerating learning at zero additional computational cost.
related_work: Experience collection in deep reinforcement learning is an open problem. While provably optimal techniques exist for tabular or bandit settings (e.g., count-based methods), they do not transfer well to high-dimensional state spaces and often introduce significant computational complexity. Consequently, practitioners still rely on simple, naive techniques like e-greedy. This paper aims to bridge the gap by introducing a theoretically motivated, computationally efficient, and simple experience collection strategy for deep RL.
abstract: Learning to make sequential decisions solely from interacting with an environment without any supervision has been achieved by the initial installation of deep neural networks as function approximators to represent and learn a value function in high-dimensional MDPs. Reinforcement learning policies face exponentially growing state spaces in experience collection in high dimensional MDPs resulting in a dichotomy between computational complexity and policy success. In our paper we focus on the agent’s interaction with the environment in a high-dimensional MDP during the learning phase and we introduce a theoretically-founded novel method based on experiences obtained through extremum actions. Our analysis and method provides a theoretical basis for effective, accelerated and efficient experience collection, and further comes with zero additional computational cost while leading to significant acceleration of training in deep reinforcement learning.

experiments.Description: To provide intuition for the proposed method in a simple, controlled environment, an experiment is planned on a toy problem called the chain MDP.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a chain MDP with n=10 states. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared with the e-greedy action selection method and the upper confidence bound (UCB) method within a tabular Q-learning framework.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing Average Cumulative Return vs. Environment Interactions.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sample efficiency of the proposed method in a low-data regime across a wide range of tasks.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) 100K benchmark. The proposed MaxMin TD Learning exploration strategy will be integrated into DDQN and QRDQN algorithms. It will be compared against two baselines: the standard e-greedy exploration strategy and NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Human normalized median scores', 'Human normalized 20th percentile scores', 'Human normalized 80th percentile scores']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed method in a standard, large-data regime with extended training.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) with 200 million frames of training (50 million environment interactions). The proposed MaxMin TD Learning algorithm will be compared against the original version of the DDQN algorithm with e-greedy training on a selection of games (e.g., StarGunner, Bowling, Surround, BankHeist, JamesBond, Amidar, Gravitar, Tennis).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning curves showing game scores vs. environment interactions.']

experiments.Description: To empirically investigate the theoretical claim that taking the minimum Q-value action results in transitions with higher temporal difference (TD).
experiments.Method: During training on the ALE 100K benchmark and the chain MDP, the temporal difference will be recorded for batches of transitions. The TD obtained by the MaxMin TD Learning method will be compared to the TD obtained by e-greedy-based learning and other baselines like NoisyNetworks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Temporal difference values', 'Normalized TD Gain, defined as 1 + (TD_method - TD_e-greedy) / |TD_e-greedy|']

title: Offline Congestion Games: How Feedback Type Affects Data Coverage Requirement
name: offline-congestion-games-feedback-types
short_hypothesis: For offline congestion games, where the action space can be exponentially large, it is possible to efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) by designing novel data coverage assumptions tailored to different types of feedback (facility-level, agent-level, and game-level), thereby avoiding the prohibitive sample complexity incurred by existing assumptions for general-sum games.
related_work: Existing theoretical work on offline multi-agent games proposes a unilateral coverage assumption for learning a Nash Equilibrium (NE). However, when applied to congestion games, this assumption results in a sample complexity that scales with the number of actions, which can be exponentially large and thus impractical. While online learning in congestion games has been studied, the offline setting, where an algorithm learns from a fixed, pre-collected dataset, is less understood, especially regarding how different types of reward feedback (e.g., per-facility, per-agent, or per-game) affect the data requirements for efficient learning.
abstract: This paper investigates when one can efficiently recover an approximate Nash Equilibrium (NE) in offline congestion games. The existing dataset coverage assumption in offline general-sum games inevitably incurs a dependency on the number of actions, which can be exponentially large in congestion games. We consider three different types of feedback with decreasing revealed information. Starting from the facility-level (a.k.a., semi-bandit) feedback, we propose a novel one-unit deviation coverage condition and show a pessimism-type algorithm that can recover an approximate NE. For the agent-level (a.k.a., bandit) feedback setting, we show the one-unit deviation coverage condition is not sufficient. On the other hand, we convert the game to multi-agent linear bandits and propose that with a generalized data coverage assumption in offline linear bandits, we can efficiently recover the approximate NE. Lastly, we consider a novel type of feedback, the game-level feedback where only the total reward from all agents is revealed. We propose that with a stronger version of the data coverage assumption for linear bandits, we can recover an approximate NE.

experiments.Description: To empirically verify the theoretical results for the facility-level feedback setting by investigating: (i) if the proposed algorithm can recover the Nash Equilibrium (NE) from datasets satisfying the proposed coverage assumption, (ii) how fast the algorithm converges as sample size increases, and (iii) how the convergence rate varies with the number of players.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a modified version of the Braess paradox, a canonical congestion game with 5 facilities (roads) and a variable number of players (m). The proposed Surrogate Minimization algorithm (Algorithm 1) is evaluated. Offline datasets are generated using two different exploration policies: 1) a `random` policy where each player selects actions uniformly, and 2) a `facility` policy designed to satisfy the proposed `Assumption 2` (one-unit deviation coverage) by covering all facility configurations achievable from unilateral deviations from the NE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance gap of the output policy, which measures its distance from being an NE. This will be plotted against the dataset sample size (n) for different numbers of players (m).']

title: Ferret-UI Lite: Lessons from Building Small On-Device GUI Agents
name: ferret-ui-lite
short_hypothesis: A compact, end-to-end 3B multimodal LLM can be built to be a competitive GUI agent for on-device deployment by curating a diverse mixture of real and synthetic GUI data, strengthening inference-time performance with chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and employing a two-stage training strategy of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards.
related_work: The majority of existing GUI agents are built on large foundation models, which are computationally expensive and have high latency, making them unsuitable for on-device deployment where low latency, privacy, and connectivity are critical. While end-to-end agents that directly map screenshots to actions are an attractive alternative, building competitive small-scale versions remains a challenge. This work explores strategies to adapt modern GUI agent techniques (SFT, synthetic data generation, CoT, RL) to the resource-constrained setting of small, on-device models.
abstract: Developing autonomous agents that effectively interact with Graphic User Interfaces (GUIs) is a challenging open problem, especially for small on-device models. This paper proposes FERRET-UI LITE, a compact, end-to-end GUI agent designed to operate across mobile, web, and desktop platforms. The proposed method involves building a 3B agent by curating a diverse GUI data mixture from real and synthetic sources, strengthening inference-time performance through chain-of-thought reasoning and visual tool-use, and applying reinforcement learning with designed rewards.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's GUI grounding performance, which is the ability to map natural language instructions to target elements on a screen.
experiments.Method: The 3B FERRET-UI LITE model will be evaluated on the ScreenSpot-V2, ScreenSpot-Pro, and OSWorld-G benchmarks. Its performance will be compared against other small-scale models like UI-R1 (3B), UITars (2B), QwenVL 2.5 (3B), and Jedi (3B), as well as larger models such as UITars (7B), GTA1 (7B), and GUI-OWL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an offline evaluation of the model's agentic planning and grounding capabilities in a mobile environment.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Android Control dataset, which features two task types: low-level tasks (single-step instructions) and high-level tasks (multi-step global goals). Performance will be compared against baselines including InternVL-2 (4B), OSAtlas (4B), and Qwen2-VL (7B).

experiments.Description: To conduct an online evaluation of the model's GUI navigation capabilities in fully functional, dynamic environments.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on two benchmarks: (1) AndroidWorld, a dynamic Android environment, with performance compared to models like QwenVL 2.5 (3B), ScaleCUA (3B), and UITars 1.5 (7B). (2) OSWorld-Verified, a benchmark for computer use simulation, with performance compared against models like ScaleCUA (3B), Kimi-VL (3B), OpenCUA (A3B), and Claude-4-Sonnet.

experiments.Description: To investigate the capabilities and limitations of small GUI agents through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be conducted to study: (1) The effect of different data mixture ratios of navigation and grounding data. (2) The impact of incorporating synthetic high-resolution data. (3) The effectiveness of synthetic Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data. (4) The contribution of SFT vs. RL training variants, including a zoom-in operation during RL. (5) The impact of different RL reward designs (action type, sparse grounding, dense grounding).

risk_factors: its performance on multi-step navigation remains limited, underscoring the inherent challenges of developing lightweight, on-device agents capable of robust long-horizon reasoning.
While small models can benefit from reinforcement learning, they are sensitive to RL reward designs, underscoring the difficulty of designing robust rewards across heterogeneous UI agentic tasks.
CoT yields structured reasoning, but only in the context of strong grounding and supervision.
Careful RL reward design is critical. Small models are highly sensitive to reward formulation.

title: Local Control Networks (LCNs): Optimizing Flexibility in Neural Network Data Pattern Capture
name: local-control-networks
short_hypothesis: Employing a uniform, fixed activation function across all nodes in a neural network is suboptimal. Using distinct, learnable activation functions at each node, specifically by leveraging B-spline functions, will increase the network's flexibility, adaptability, and representational capacity, enabling it to model complex data patterns more effectively.
related_work: Traditional Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLPs) use a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all neurons, which can limit flexibility and lead to issues like 'dead neurons' or vanishing gradients. More recent architectures like Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have introduced learnable activation functions on the edges of the network, offering increased flexibility but facing limitations in application and complexity. The proposed Local Control Networks (LCNs) aim to bridge this gap by enabling each neuron to have its own unique, learnable B-spline activation function within a standard network structure, offering a higher degree of flexibility and local adaptability without the architectural complexity of KANs.
abstract: The widespread use of multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) often relies on a fixed activation function (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) for all nodes within the hidden layers. While effective in many scenarios, this uniformity may limit the network's ability to capture complex data patterns. We argue that employing the same activation function at every node is suboptimal and propose leveraging different activation functions at each node to increase flexibility and adaptability. To achieve this, we introduce Local Control Networks (LCNs), which leverage B-spline functions to enable distinct activation curves at each node.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed LCN model on a variety of basic machine learning tasks and compare it against standard baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Bank Marketing, Bean Classification, Spam Detection, and Telescope datasets. The proposed LCN model will be compared against conventional Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) with fixed activations and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs). To ensure a fair comparison, the total number of parameters will be standardized across all models, and LCN and KAN models will undergo hyperparameter tuning via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance and scalability of LCNs on computer vision tasks involving high-dimensional input spaces.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST (FMNIST) datasets for image classification. The LCN model's performance will be compared to that of MLPs and KANs. Model configurations will be standardized by adjusting layers and neurons to have a comparable number of total parameters, with hyperparameters for LCN and KAN being tuned via grid search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of LCNs on symbolic regression tasks, which involve approximating mathematical functions.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on synthetic datasets designed for symbolic regression. The LCN model will be benchmarked against MLPs and KANs. The total number of parameters will be standardized across models for a fair comparison.

risk_factors: The marginal improvements of LCNs over MLPs and KANs reflect the simplicity of the datasets used, which follow the standards in (Yu et al., 2024).
On these basic tasks, MLPs already achieve strong performance, leaving limited room for improvement for LCNs and KANs.
However, the flexibility and localized activations of LCNs are expected to show greater advantages on more complex datasets with higher-dimensional inputs or intricate patterns.

title: VAE Approximation Error: ELBO and Exponential Families
name: vae-approximation-error-exponential-families
short_hypothesis: For Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) where both the encoder and decoder are conditional exponential families, the ELBO objective inherently pulls the learned decoder away from the maximum likelihood solution towards a restricted subclass of 'consistent' models (which are RBM-like). This approximation error cannot be reduced by simply using deeper neural networks for the encoder and decoder.
related_work: The VAE approximation error, or the gap between the true and approximate posterior, has been studied empirically. Many works have proposed mitigating this gap by using more expressive encoders, such as those with normalizing flows, hierarchical structures, or autoregressive models. However, these extensions often increase training difficulty and inference cost. There is a lack of theoretical understanding of the coupling between the decoder and the chosen encoder family, particularly for the common case where both are modeled as exponential families. This paper aims to theoretically analyze this coupling and its effect on the learned decoder.
abstract: The importance of Variational Autoencoders reaches far beyond standalone generative models — the approach is also used for learning latent representations and can be generalized to semi-supervised learning. This requires a thorough analysis of their commonly known shortcomings: posterior collapse and approximation errors. This paper analyzes VAE approximation errors caused by the combination of the ELBO objective and encoder models from conditional exponential families, including, but not limited to, commonly used conditionally independent discrete and continuous models. We propose to characterize subclasses of generative models consistent with these encoder families. We hypothesize that the ELBO optimizer is pulled away from the likelihood optimizer towards this consistent subset. Importantly, this subset can not be enlarged, and the respective error cannot be decreased, by considering deeper encoder/decoder networks.

experiments.Description: To illustrate the theoretical findings on a toy example by studying the influence of the encoder's factorization assumption on the learned model when the ground truth is a known Gaussian mixture model.
experiments.Method: A VAE will be trained on data from a ground truth Gaussian mixture model (4 components in 2D). The decoder will use a Gaussian distribution, and the encoder will be a factorized model over the binary representation of the latent code, implemented as a 2-layer MLP. Training will first involve pre-training the encoder with a fixed ground truth decoder, followed by joint optimization of the encoder and decoder to maximize ELBO. The resulting model posterior will be compared to the true posterior and the posterior of an RBM trained on the same data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of ELBO optimization on a realistic image generation task using the CelebA dataset.
experiments.Method: A ground truth generative model will be created by training a convolutional GAN on 64x64 CelebA images and adding Gaussian noise to its output. A VAE with a decoder architecture matching the GAN generator and a convolutional encoder will be trained. First, the encoder will be pre-trained by maximizing the conditional log-likelihood on samples from the ground truth model. Then, the encoder and decoder will be jointly trained by maximizing ELBO, starting from the pre-trained components. Two training variants will be explored: one with fixed image noise and one where the noise is a learnable parameter.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Frechet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Visual quality of generated images']

experiments.Description: To compare a proposed theoretically-motivated encoder design against standard practices for the semantic hashing of text documents.
experiments.Method: A Bernoulli VAE model for semantic hashing will be trained on the 20Newsgroups dataset. Three encoder variants will be compared: (e1) a linear encoder on raw word counts (the proposed correction), (e2) a deep 2-layer encoder on word frequencies, and (e3) a linear encoder on word frequencies. These encoders will be evaluated across different latent code sizes (8, 16, 32, 64 bits) and decoder depths (0, 1, 2 hidden layers).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Negative ELBO (NELBO) on training and test sets']

risk_factors: We restricted this study to exponential families. While in richer models discussed in the introduction, the approximation error still exists, it is made small by design, and it is less relevant and harder to analyze it theoretically.
The error is detrimental when the consistent subset is too restrictive.
The VAE approximation error may result in a useful regularization when the respective RBM is a good baseline model.

title: Diffusion SigFormer for Interference Time-series Signal Recognition
name: diffusion-sigformer
short_hypothesis: A novel interference signal recognition transformer, Diffusion SigFormer, which combines a diffusion signal denoising module with a transformer-convolution hybrid (SigFormer), can effectively recognize and classify interference time-series signals.
related_work: The various interferences in the actual environment make electromagnetic signal recognition challenging, creating a need for methods with robust anti-interference capabilities.
abstract: The various interferences in the actual environment make electromagnetic signal recognition a challenging but important task. To address this, a novel interference signal recognition transformer, named Diffusion SigFormer, is proposed. The method first uses a diffusion signal denoising module to denoise the input interference signal. Then, a model named SigFormer, which leverages 1-D Patch Embedding and combines a transformer with convolution, is designed to extract features from and classify the denoised signal.

experiments.Description: To verify the anti-interference ability and recognition performance of the proposed Diffusion SigFormer method.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated on the RML2016.10a, RML2016.10b, and BT datasets.

title: The Surprising Effectiveness of Equivariant Models in Domains with Latent Symmetry
name: Extrinsic Equivariance for Latent Symmetries
short_hypothesis: This paper tests the hypothesis that if a model is constrained to a symmetry class which is related to the true, but unknown, problem symmetry, then it may help learn a model that satisfies the true symmetry. Specifically, imposing equivariance constraints that do not exactly match the domain symmetry (termed 'extrinsic equivariance') can be helpful in learning the true symmetry in the environment and can improve performance over unconstrained models.
related_work: Previous work on equivariant neural networks typically assumes that the domain symmetry is perfectly known and can be explicitly modeled. In contrast, this work addresses domains with latent or partial symmetries where the action of the symmetry group on the input is not precisely known. While other methods learn symmetric representations, this work proposes to directly use an equivariant network as an encoder, even when the equivariance constraint is 'imprecise'. This extends prior work on equivariant reinforcement learning, which is mostly limited to fully equivariant domains, to tasks with latent symmetries.
abstract: Equivariant neural networks can improve sample efficiency by enforcing an inductive bias, but this typically assumes domain symmetry is fully described by explicit transformations. However, many real-life applications contain only latent or partial symmetries which cannot be easily described by simple transformations of the input. In these cases, it is necessary to learn symmetry in the environment instead of imposing it mathematically. This work proposes that imposing equivariance constraints that do not exactly match the domain symmetry can be helpful in learning the true symmetry. We differentiate between 'extrinsic' and 'incorrect' symmetry constraints, and propose that imposing extrinsic symmetry can improve performance.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effect of different types of mismatched model symmetries (extrinsic vs. incorrect) on the performance of an equivariant network in a supervised learning context.
experiments.Method: The task is a supervised learning problem to classify the relative rotation (from group C8) between two images of three ducks. The experiment will introduce various 'symmetry corruptions' such as grid, occlusion, light effect, squeeze, reflect, side, and invert-label. The primary model is an equivariant network (Equi) built with e2cnn. It will be compared against a conventional CNN model with a similar parameter count, and a CNN model augmented with C8 image rotations.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that enforcing extrinsic equivariance can improve sample efficiency in reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation tasks with latent symmetries.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses five robotic manipulation environments from the BulletArm benchmark (Block Pulling, Block Pushing, Block Picking, Drawer Opening, Block in Bowl) with skewed-perspective RGBD image inputs, creating latent O(2) symmetry. The proposed method is Equivariant SAC (enforcing D4 symmetry) with random crop augmentation (RAD). It will be compared against several baselines: CNN SAC + RAD, CNN SAC + DrQ, FERM, and SEN + RAD.

experiments.Description: To investigate how the performance of an extrinsically equivariant network is affected by increasing the degree of symmetry corruption, i.e., the gap between the model's image-space transform and the true object-space transform.
experiments.Method: Using robotic manipulation environments, the camera view angle will be varied from 90 degrees (top-down, perfect symmetry) to 15 degrees (highly skewed, large corruption). The performance of Equivariant SAC + RAD will be compared against CNN SAC + RAD across these different angles.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Discounted reward at the end of training']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate a scenario where enforcing an 'incorrect' equivariance constraint harms the performance of an equivariant RL agent compared to an unconstrained one.
experiments.Method: The robotic manipulation environments are modified to apply random reflections (vertical and horizontal) to the state image at each step. This is designed to turn the extrinsic D4 symmetry constraint of Equivariant SAC into an incorrect one. The performance of Equivariant SAC will be compared to a standard CNN-based SAC.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether enforcing extrinsic equivariance can improve training performance on standard continuous control benchmarks with inherent object-level symmetries but corrupted visual inputs.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a subset of tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite (cartpole, cup catch, pendulum, acrobot, reacher) which have object-level symmetry but visual corruptions (e.g., checkered floors, random backgrounds). An equivariant version of the DrQv2 algorithm will be created and compared against the original non-equivariant DrQv2. The model will enforce D1 or D2 group symmetry depending on the task.

risk_factors: A limitation of this work is that we mainly experiment in reinforcement learning and a simple supervised setting but not in other domains where equivariant learning is widely used.
Another limitation is that we focus on planar equivariant networks.

title: Sqrt(d) Dimension Dependence of Langevin Monte Carlo
name: lmc-sqrt-d-dependence
short_hypothesis: The mixing time of unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) for sampling from log-smooth and log-strongly-convex distributions has a dimension dependence of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d})$, an improvement over the existing $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d)$ bounds. This can be achieved under an additional growth condition on the third-order derivative of the potential, without requiring momentum as in kinetic Langevin methods.
related_work: The best known mixing time bound for unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) in 2-Wasserstein distance is $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/\epsilon)$. In contrast, methods like Metropolis-Adjusted LMC (MALA) and discretized kinetic Langevin dynamics have been shown to achieve a better $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d})$ dependence. It is an open question whether unadjusted LMC can also achieve this improved dimension dependence, as it was previously thought that the momentum term in kinetic methods was necessary for this improvement.
abstract: This article considers the popular MCMC method of unadjusted Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC) and provides a non-asymptotic analysis of its sampling error in 2-Wasserstein distance. The motivation is to improve upon the best previously known $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/\epsilon)$ mixing time bound. The proposed approach is based on a refinement of mean-square analysis, which is used to automate the analysis of a large class of sampling algorithms based on discretizations of contractive SDEs. Using this framework, we aim to establish an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d}/\epsilon)$ mixing time bound for LMC under common log-smooth and log-strongly-convex conditions, plus a growth condition on the 3rd-order derivative of the potential.

experiments.Description: To numerically study the dependence of LMC's sampling error on the problem dimension d.
experiments.Method: Run LMC with a fixed step size (h=0.1) for 100 iterations on two target potential functions, $f_1(x) = \frac{1}{2}||x||^2 + \log(\sum_{i=1}^{d}e^{x_i})$ and $f_2(x) = \frac{1}{2}||x||^2 - \frac{1}{2d^{1/2}}\sum_{i=1}^{d}\cos(d^{1/4}x_i)$. The experiment will be run for a range of dimensions $d \in \{1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000\}$. The ground truth mean is to be approximated by running LMC with a very small step size for a long time.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Euclidean norm of the mean error, $||\\mathbb{E}\\overline{x}_{k}-\\mathbb{E}_{\\mu}x||$, as a surrogate for the 2-Wasserstein distance.']

experiments.Description: To numerically study the dependence of LMC's sampling error on the algorithm's step size h.
experiments.Method: Run LMC with a fixed dimension (d=10) on the same two target potential functions, $f_1(x)$ and $f_2(x)$. The experiment will be run for a range of step sizes $h \in \{0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0\}$. The algorithm will run until a fixed time T=20.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Euclidean norm of the mean error, $||\\mathbb{E}\\overline{x}_{k}-\\mathbb{E}_{\\mu}x||$, as a surrogate for the 2-Wasserstein distance.']

risk_factors: Note that the above optimality results only partially, but not completely, close the gap between the upper and lower bounds of LMC over the entire family of log-smooth and log-strongly-convex target measures, because of one limitation of our result — A(ssumption)2 is, despite of its close relation to the Hessian Lipschitz condition frequently used in the literature, still an extra condition.

We tend to believe that A2 may not be essential, but rather an artifact of our proof technique. However, at this moment we cannot eliminate the possibility that the best scaling one can get out of Assumption 1 only (no A2) is worse than $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{d}/\epsilon)$
We also note KLMC has better condition number dependence than our current LMC result, although the κ dependence in our bound may not be tight.

title: A Modulation Layer to Increase Neural Network Robustness Against Data Quality Issues
name: Modulation-Layer-for-Data-Quality
short_hypothesis: A novel neural network modification, inspired by biological neuromodulation, can mitigate the impacts of missing data. This is achieved by replacing the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (e.g., a reliability score), which is jointly learned with the main task using a multi-layer perceptron. This allows the network to up- and down-weight inputs based on their quality.
related_work: Prior work on handling missing data includes various imputation methods, from simple techniques like mean imputation to more complex ones like chained equations and deep learning-based generative models (e.g., GANs, VAEs). Another line of work incorporates uncertainty into deep networks using Bayesian methods or Gaussian processes. However, these approaches can be complex, assumption-laden, or may not leverage meaningful missingness patterns effectively. The proposed modulation layer offers a more direct mechanism to handle data quality issues by dynamically adjusting input weights based on reliability, which is distinct from imputation or complex probabilistic modeling.
abstract: Data quality is a common problem in machine learning, especially in high-stakes settings such as healthcare. Missing data affects accuracy, calibration, and feature attribution in complex patterns. While some classifiers naturally handle missing data, others, such as deep neural networks, are not designed for unknown values. Making machine learning models robust to missing data is therefore crucial for practical applications. We propose a novel neural network modification to mitigate the impacts of missing data. The approach is inspired by neuromodulation that is performed by biological neural networks. Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data. The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multi-layer perceptron.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the proposed Modulated Fully Connected Layer (MFCL and MFCL+) in classification tasks where missing value flags are used as the modulating signal.
experiments.Method: The experiments are run on the ACTFAST dataset (for 30-day Mortality, Acute Kidney Injury, and Heart Attack prediction) and the Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset. The proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers are tested in the place of fully-connected layers at the input level of MLP architectures. Baselines include MLPs fed with imputed values from various algorithms (chained regression with Bayesian ridge, missForest, GAIN, VAEAC), a graphical network model (GRAPE), and an MLP with mean imputation and a concatenated missingness indicator (FC+Mod).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area under receiver operating curve (AUROC)', 'Area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC)']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the modulation layer when using input reliability (quantified by noise level) as the modulating signal, instead of binary missing flags.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses the complete Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset. Gaussian noise with a variable standard deviation (SD) is added to the input features. The SD value is used as the modulating reliability signal for the proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers. The models are compared against the same baseline architectures used in the other classification tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area under receiver operating curve (AUROC)', 'Area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC)']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the MFCL and MFCL+ layers for data imputation within an autoencoder architecture.
experiments.Method: The experiments use the ACTFAST, Breast Cancer, and Boston datasets. The MFCL and MFCL+ layers are added in place of standard fully-connected layers at the input of an autoencoder imputation system. The baseline is a standard autoencoder with fully-connected layers.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of the proposed MFCL and MFCL+ layers in a regression task with missing data.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses the Boston housing dataset. The MFCL and MFCL+ layers are added in place of fully-connected layers at the input of MLP networks, using missing flags as the modulating signal. Baselines are two MLPs: one with input imputed via chained regression (Iterative Imputer) and another using constant value imputation with a concatenated missingness indicator (FC+Mod).

risk_factors: One other limitation is the lack of availability of large open tabular datasets with high missingness which limits the ability to generalize our findings.
To make our experiments with informative missingness comparable across features, we restricted our input space to numeric variables and discarded categorical variables.
We tested the application of the modulation process only in fully connected layers which are limited by nature in the types of data that they can handle.
We did not search over regularization strategies of the modulation network, which may further improve its performance.
Preliminary experiments placing MFCL layers deep in the autoencoder experiments did not yield visible improvement (data not shown).

title: Fast Training Dataset Attribution via In-Context Learning
name: fast-tda-via-icl
short_hypothesis: The contribution of training datasets to the outputs of instruction-tuned LLMs can be estimated without access to model internals by using in-context learning. We propose two methods: 1) A similarity-based approach (Shapley Context Method - SCM) which posits that providing a dataset as context to an LLM trained on it changes its output less than for an LLM not trained on it. 2) A mixture distribution model approach (Context Mixture Factorization - CMF) that frames attribution as a matrix factorization problem to separate a base model component from dataset-specific components.
related_work: Existing Training Data Attribution (TDA) methods are primarily either retraining-based or influence function-based. Retraining-based methods are computationally expensive as they require retraining the model without the target data. Influence function-based methods are more efficient but rely on a linear approximation of the model which can be inaccurate. Critically, both approaches require access to the internal workings of the model (e.g., gradients), which is not feasible for many popular black-box LLMs.
abstract: We investigate the use of in-context learning and prompt engineering to estimate the contributions of training data in the outputs of instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs). We propose two novel approaches: (1) a similarity-based approach that measures the difference between LLM outputs with and without provided context, and (2) a mixture distribution model approach that frames the problem of identifying contribution scores as a matrix factorization task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the proposed attribution methods (SCM and CMF) to identify the influence of different datasets with varying levels of exposure (seen, unseen but similar, and novel) on LLM outputs.
experiments.Method: The experiment will apply SCM and CMF to a set of instruction-tuned LLMs (GPT-4, Bloomz, Mistral 7B, Phi-3-mini). The evaluation will be conducted on a binary Q&A task using three primary datasets: BoolQ (likely seen during pretraining), FakeQ (synthetically created and unseen), and Olympic 2024 (guaranteed unseen). Five additional Wikipedia science domain datasets will be used as broad controls. The performance will be compared against the Trak attribution baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Attribution scores from SCM (Shapley values) and CMF (π coefficients).', 'The primary evaluation is based on the expectation that attribution scores will be highest for BoolQ, lower for FakeQ, and lowest for Olympic2024, reflecting the degree of data exposure.']

experiments.Description: To validate that the proposed attribution metrics can reliably capture a model's growing dependence on a dataset by measuring attribution during a controlled fine-tuning process.
experiments.Method: LLMs will be incrementally fine-tuned on the previously unseen Olympic2024 dataset using progressively increasing learning rates. After each fine-tuning step, the SCM and CMF attribution methods will be applied to measure the contribution of the Olympic2024 dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The attribution scores from SCM and CMF for the Olympic2024 dataset.', 'The metrics are considered valid if the attribution scores increase monotonically with the extent of fine-tuning.']

experiments.Description: To assess the utility of the proposed attribution metrics for evaluating and ranking the effectiveness of different machine unlearning algorithms.
experiments.Method: Three machine unlearning methods (Gradient Ascent, Fine-tuning with Random Labels, Unlearning with Adversarial Samples) will be applied to Bloomz and Phi-3 models that have been fine-tuned on the Olympic2024 dataset. The goal is to make the models 'unlearn' this dataset. The SCM and CMF attribution methods will be used to measure the influence of the Olympic2024 dataset before and after the unlearning process.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The reduction in attribution scores (from SCM and CMF) for the Olympic2024 dataset.', 'A larger reduction in the attribution score indicates a more effective unlearning method.']

risk_factors: Given the modularity of LLM structures, this assumption [that an LLM's behavior can be modeled as a mixture of a general-purpose model and specialized models for each dataset] is not fully realistic.
Model (3) can capture the scenarios where an LLM uses data from multiple sources, but does not model the scenarios where the LLM uses the interaction of data from multiple sources.
While we focus on BoolQ-style binary QA for tractability, our framework is general and can extend to multi-class or free-form generation. Future work will apply SCM and CMF to such outputs...
Retrieval design itself (e.g., chunk size, reranking, or long-context strategies) merits study, as it shapes the stability of attribution.

title: BESPOKE: Benchmark for Search-Augmented Large Language Model Personalization via Diagnostic Feedback
name: bespoke-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A new benchmark, BESPOKE, constructed from long-term, authentic human chat and search histories paired with fine-grained diagnostic feedback, will enable a more realistic and systematic evaluation of personalization in search-augmented LLMs. An LLM-based evaluator using this data can be designed to effectively assess personalization quality along multiple dimensions.
related_work: Current evaluation methods for search-augmented LLMs, like RAG-QA Arena and Search Arena, primarily focus on factuality and general preference judgments, but neglect personalization. Existing personalized LLM evaluation benchmarks, such as LaMP-QA, rely on data from constrained domains like StackExchange (QA-style interactions) or use synthetic personas, which do not reflect realistic, long-term, and diverse user activities. This creates a gap for a benchmark that can systematically evaluate personalization in systems like ChatGPT and Gemini, which leverage rich user histories composed of both chat and search interactions.
abstract: Search-augmented large language models (LLMs) remain insufficient for fully addressing diverse user needs, where the same query can reflect different intents across users. While recent systems like ChatGPT and Gemini attempt personalization by leveraging user histories, systematic evaluation of this capability is under-explored. To address this gap, we propose BESPOKE, a realistic benchmark for evaluating personalization in search-augmented LLMs. BESPOKE is designed to be both realistic, by collecting authentic chat and search histories directly from humans, and diagnostic, by pairing responses with fine-grained preference scores and feedback. The benchmark is constructed through long-term, deeply engaged human annotation, where annotators contribute their own histories, author queries, and evaluate responses with scores and diagnostic feedback.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed LLM-based evaluator (Ep) by assessing its alignment with human judgments on personalization quality.
experiments.Method: A meta-evaluation dataset of 300 Response-Judgment (R-J) pairs is used. The proposed GPT-5-based evaluator, which uses both scores and feedback as context ('w/ Feedback'), is compared against two baselines: a generic evaluator without personalization context ('w/o Personal.') and an evaluator that uses only scores as context ('w/o Feedback').
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Score Alignment (Pearson/Spearman correlation)', 'Feedback Alignment (Accuracy of a meta-evaluator judging semantic equivalence)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of leveraging user history on the personalization performance of search-augmented LLMs and to determine the most effective method for constructing user context.
experiments.Method: Six search-augmented LLMs (GPT-4o-search, o3-search, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Perplexity-sonar, Perplexity-sonar-reasoning) are evaluated on the BESPOKE benchmark. The experiment varies four aspects of user context construction: 1) history usage (with vs. without), 2) query awareness (static vs. dynamic context), 3) history selection (full vs. query-relevant), and 4) context form (raw vs. profile).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Personalization Scores (Need Alignment, Content Depth, Tone, Explanation Style)', 'Gold Information Coverage (Recall)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate efficient methods for retrieving relevant user history sessions for personalization, comparing query expansion techniques against a baseline.
experiments.Method: A bi-encoder retriever (Stella-V5-1.5B) is used for history retrieval on the BESPOKE dataset. The performance of the original user query is compared against two query expansion methods: CoT (Chain-of-Thought) expansion and Pseudo-history expansion. Ground truth is established by designating oracle histories using an LLM.

experiments.Description: To isolate the impact of web-search quality on the personalization of model responses.
experiments.Method: An experiment is designed where the model's built-in search tool is disabled and replaced with the ground-truth 'gold information' from the BESPOKE dataset. This condition ('Personalized + Gold Information') is compared against the standard setting where models use their own web search ('Personalized').

experiments.Description: To investigate how the performance of reasoning and non-reasoning models changes as the quality of retrieved information deteriorates.
experiments.Method: A controlled comparison is conducted using Sonar and Sonar-Reasoning models. Noise is systematically injected into the gold information provided to the models at varying ratios (0%, 30%, 50%, 70%) to simulate deteriorating search quality.

risk_factors: While our dataset is relatively small in scale due to its reliance on deeply engaged human annotation, it provides rich user histories that reflect authentic and realistic preferences.

While our GPT-5-based evaluator shows strong empirical performance, this reliance on a proprietary model has two important limitations. It may reduce generalizability across model families, and it incurs non-trivial computational cost for running the benchmark end-to-end.
While leveraging LLMs can directly assess query-history relevance as discussed in Section 4.2, they are prohibitively costly and impractical to scale.
Despite these advances, none of the models in any setting surpass an average score of 60. This ceiling highlights that personalization in realistic environments remains a significant challenge.

title: RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code
name: refactorbench-for-stateful-agents
short_hypothesis: Current language model agents, framed as Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs), struggle with long-horizon, multi-file code refactoring tasks because their understanding of the environment's state weakens over many steps. Introducing a "state-aware" interface that explicitly provides agents with a summary of their past actions and the resulting state changes will improve their reasoning capabilities and task success rate.
related_work: Prior work on evaluating software engineering agents has relied on benchmarks like SWE-bench, which focuses on resolving GitHub issues, or function-level code generation benchmarks. These benchmarks do not specifically isolate the challenges of multi-file code refactoring, which requires stateful reasoning across a sequence of edits. While some research has augmented agents with external memory to handle long-term dependencies, this work focuses on enabling concurrent reasoning and state-aware behaviors by providing agents with explicit state representations, a concept explored in embodied control but less so for real-world language agents interacting with code.
abstract: To better understand the unique limitations of language model (LM) agents in autonomous, feedback-driven systems, we introduce RefactorBench, a benchmark consisting of 100 large handcrafted multi-file refactoring tasks in popular open-source repositories. Solving tasks within RefactorBench requires thorough exploration of dependencies across multiple files and strong adherence to relevant instructions. Every task is defined by 3 natural language instructions of varying specificity. We propose adapting a baseline agent to condition on representations of state to improve performance on these tasks. We further propose extending this state-aware approach to encompass entire digital environments. RefactorBench aims to support the study of LM agents by providing a set of real-world, multi-hop tasks within the realm of code.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of baseline language model agents on the newly proposed multi-file code refactoring benchmark and analyze their failure modes.
experiments.Method: A baseline of the SWE-agent with gpt-4 and claude-3.5-sonnet will be run on all tasks in the RefactorBench benchmark. RefactorBench consists of 100 handcrafted multi-file refactoring tasks in 9 popular Python repositories. Each task is defined by three instruction sets with varying levels of detail: 'lazy', 'base', and 'descriptive'. A proficient human developer with a 5-minute time limit per task will also be evaluated as a baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task resolution rate (percentage of completely successful task instances)', 'Success is determined by passing all associated Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) based unit tests for a given task.', 'Manual review of trajectories to identify failure modes.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that a language model's ability to track state degrades as the number of actions taken increases.
experiments.Method: A synthetic experiment will be conducted where a language model (GPT-4 Turbo) is given an initial state consisting of 15 categories of preferences. The model will then be prompted with an increasing number of updates (actions) to these preferences and asked to output the final, updated list of preferences after each sequence of actions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy of the final constructed state (the list of preferences) at each step, measured over 125 randomly initialized runs.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether augmenting a language model agent with a state-aware interface improves its performance on RefactorBench.
experiments.Method: The SWE-agent, using gpt-4o, will be modified to include a 'state-aware interface'. This interface provides the agent with a cached and updated summary of all previous edit actions before every function call. This state-aware agent will be evaluated on the RefactorBench tasks across all three instruction sets (lazy, base, descriptive) and its performance will be compared to the baseline SWE-agent.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task resolution rate', 'Subtask completion rate (based on passing individual AST tests)']

risk_factors: RefactorBench's task instances are all in Python, and we hope to expand the benchmark to various languages that are statically and dynamically typed, allowing for evaluations on more styles of refactors.
We also focus on highly used open-source Python repositories, and language models may have a better understanding of the repositories due to their prevalence in training data.

RefactorBench also has a limited amount of task instances due to the intensive process to create a singular end-to-end task and the necessity for quick evaluations (evaluations still takes hours with RefactorBench).
We also find, similar to previous works, that agent runs are not deterministic and can solve differing tasks in different runs.

RefactorBench is a step forward in evaluating LM agents in robust manners through complex task construction, but like all benchmarks, is still plagued by the possible issues of over-fit data distributions (i.e. only refactors).

title: Robustness May be More Brittle than We Think under Different Degrees of Distribution Shifts
name: brittle-robustness-under-varying-shifts
short_hypothesis: Evaluating models across a continuous spectrum of distribution shift severities, rather than at fixed points, will reveal that model robustness is brittle and inconsistent. A model's performance under a mild shift may not predict its performance under a slightly stronger shift. Furthermore, large-scale pre-trained models like CLIP may be surprisingly sensitive to even minute distribution shifts on novel downstream tasks.
related_work: Most Out-of-Distribution (OOD) benchmarks like WILDS and DomainBed incorporate multiple datasets to cover diverse *types* of distribution shifts. However, they often overlook the *degree* of shift, typically evaluating on a fixed severity for each shift type. This can lead to biased conclusions about a model's OOD performance. Similarly, while foundation models like CLIP have shown remarkable robustness, it is unclear how their robustness transfers to downstream tasks and how they behave as the degree of a downstream distribution shift increases.
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization is a complicated problem due to the idiosyncrasies of possible distribution shifts between training and test domains. Most benchmarks employ diverse datasets to address this issue; however, the degree of the distribution shift between the training domains and the test domains of each dataset remains largely fixed. This may lead to biased conclusions that either underestimate or overestimate the actual OOD performance of a model. This study proposes to delve into a more nuanced evaluation setting that covers a broad range of shift degrees to investigate if model robustness is brittle and inconsistent under different degrees of distribution shifts. Additionally, the study will examine whether large-scale pre-trained models, such as CLIP, are sensitive to even minute distribution shifts of novel downstream tasks, which could indicate that pre-training may have minimal or even adverse effects on generalization in certain OOD scenarios.

experiments.Description: To investigate if model robustness extrapolates from milder to stronger distribution shifts by training models on mildly shifted data and evaluating them across a full spectrum of shift severities.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on image classification tasks using the NOISYMNIST, ROTATEDMNIST, and LOWLIGHTCIFAR10 datasets. The model architectures will be a simple 4-layer CNN and a ResNet-50, trained from scratch. These will be compared against a baseline of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) and over 20 domain generalization (DG) algorithms, including IRM, VREx, GroupDRO, CORAL, and DANN.

experiments.Description: To investigate the reverse question: whether robustness to strong distribution shifts guarantees robustness to milder shifts. This involves training models on strongly shifted data and evaluating them across all milder degrees of shift.
experiments.Method: ERM models (Simple CNN, ResNet-50) will be trained on the NOISYMNIST and ROTATEDMNIST datasets using various combinations of training domains, such as clean data plus strongly shifted data (e.g., D0 and D10). These will be compared to models trained only on clean data or on clean data plus mildly shifted data.

experiments.Description: To investigate how pre-training influences the OOD generalization behavior of models on downstream tasks under multiple shift degrees, and to test if large-scale pre-trained models are sensitive to novel downstream shifts.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use NOISYMNIST, ROTATEDMNIST, NOISYIMAGENET15, and LR-IMAGENET15 datasets. Pre-trained models including ImageNet pre-trained ResNet-50, ViT-B/32, and their CLIP-trained counterparts will be adapted to the downstream tasks via linear probing. These will be compared against randomly initialized ResNet-50 and ViT-B/32 models trained from scratch on the same tasks.

risk_factors: Although we focus on image classification problems and use accuracy as the metric for measuring model performance, we believe that the conclusions drawn from our experiment results also hold for more general problems.

title: Multi-Action Self-Improvement For Neural Combinatorial Optimization
name: multi-action-self-improvement-nco
short_hypothesis: By extending self-improvement to operate over joint multi-agent actions, where a model predicts complete agent-task assignments jointly at each step and is trained with a set-prediction loss, it is possible to explicitly leverage agent-permutation symmetries. This approach is hypothesized to enhance sample efficiency, improve the ability to learn coordinated behavior, and accelerate solution generation compared to standard single-action self-improvement methods.
related_work: Self-improvement has become a state-of-the-art paradigm in Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO), where models learn by imitating high-quality pseudo-expert solutions they generate. However, existing methods are based on next-token prediction, which is fundamentally limited for multi-agent problems that exhibit agent-permutation symmetries (e.g., vehicle routing, scheduling). By supervising on a single 'best' next action, these approaches implicitly enforce an arbitrary agent order, treating symmetric choices as errors. This reduces sample efficiency and hinders the model's ability to learn coordinated behavior. While other methods like POMO or DPN exploit symmetries, they do so via specialized baselines rather than changing the action generation and learning objective itself.
abstract: Self-improvement has emerged as a state-of-the-art paradigm in Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO), but existing methods are computationally expensive and fail to exploit the structure of multi-agent combinatorial problems. By supervising on single-action trajectories, they fail to exploit agent-permutation symmetries, where distinct sequences of actions yield identical solutions, hindering generalization and the ability to learn coordinated behavior. This work addresses these challenges by extending self-improvement to operate over joint multi-agent actions. The proposed model architecture, MACSIM, predicts complete agent-task assignments jointly at each decision step. To explicitly leverage symmetries, a set-prediction loss is employed to supervise the policy on multiple expert assignments for any given state.
