This approach is designed to enhance sample efficiency, the ability to learn coordinated behavior, and accelerate the solution generation phase of the self-improvement loop.

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of the proposed MACSIM method on the Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP), a challenging multi-agent scheduling task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on FJSP instances. It will be compared against a state-of-the-art CP-SAT solver (OR-Tools), classical priority dispatching rules (FIFO, MOR, MWKR), other learning-based approaches (PPO, DANIEL), and a standard self-improvement baseline (SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of MACSIM on the Flexible Flow Shop Problem (FFSP), another representative multi-agent scheduling task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on FFSP instances. It will be compared against traditional baselines (Gurobi, Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization) and neural baselines (MatNet, PolyNet, PARCO, SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of MACSIM on the Min-Max Heterogeneous Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (HCVRP), a multi-agent routing problem.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate MACSIM on HCVRP instances. It will be compared against heuristic baselines (Simulated Annealing, SISR) and other neural baselines (Equity-Transformer (ET), DRLLi, 2D-Ptr, DPN, SLIM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average objective value (Obj.)', 'Gap to best-known solutions', 'Average inference latency']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization performance of MACSIM by testing models trained on small FJSP instances on larger, unseen instance distributions.
experiments.Method: Models trained on small FJSP instances will be evaluated on larger instances. MACSIM will be compared against OR-Tools, PPO, DANIEL, and SLIM.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to assess the impact of MACSIM's key components, specifically the autoregressive sampling procedure and the skip token.
experiments.Method: On the FJSP task, the full MACSIM model will be compared against variants using different sequence generation modes (random-order, fixed-order) and a variant without the skip token.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed permutation-invariant surrogate loss function against alternative loss formulations.
experiments.Method: On FJSP and FFSP tasks, the performance of MACSIM trained with the proposed set-based cross-entropy loss (L_CE) will be compared to training with a single-agent cross-entropy loss (L_SA), a multi-agent Maximum Likelihood Estimation loss (L_ML), and a Plackett-Luce loss (L_PL).

risk_factors: Although MACSIM substantially improves inference and training time compared to standard self-improvement, its reliance on step-wise encoding results in slower generation than some lightweight neural solvers, which may hinder applicability in latency-critical settings.

title: Listen, Think, and Understand
name: ltu-audio-language-model
short_hypothesis: By integrating a high-performance audio perception model with a large language model and training it on a newly created, large-scale audio question-answering dataset (OpenAQA-5M) using a perception-to-understanding curriculum, it is possible to create a single model that possesses both audio perception and emerging reasoning and comprehension abilities.
related_work: Existing audio models, while proficient at perception tasks like classifying sounds into predefined categories, lack reasoning and understanding capabilities. For example, they can recognize a clock chime but not infer the time. Conversely, modern large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning abilities and possess knowledge about audio, but they cannot perceive audio signals directly. This creates a gap where no single model effectively combines audio perception with deep reasoning. This work aims to bridge that gap by creating a multimodal large language model focused on general audio understanding, unlike prior work that has concentrated on visual or speech modalities.
abstract: The ability of artificial intelligence (AI) systems to perceive and comprehend audio signals is crucial for many applications. Although significant progress has been made, most existing models are designed to map audio inputs to pre-defined, discrete sound label sets. In contrast, humans possess the ability to not only classify sounds but also to listen to finer details, explain predictions, think about what the sound infers, and understand the scene. Such capabilities beyond perception are not yet present in existing audio models. On the other hand, modern large language models (LLMs) exhibit emerging reasoning ability but they lack audio perception capabilities. Therefore, we ask the question: can we build a model that has both audio perception and reasoning ability? In this paper, we propose a new audio foundation model, called LTU (Listen, Think, and Understand). To train LTU, we created a new OpenAQA-5M dataset consisting of 1.9 million closed-ended and 3.7 million open-ended, diverse (audio, question, answer) tuples, and have used an autoregressive training framework with a perception-to-understanding curriculum.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's perception ability on a variety of classic closed-ended audio classification tasks across multiple benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The proposed LTU model will be evaluated on eight audio classification benchmarks: ESC-50, DCASE2017 Task 4, VocalSound, TUT 2017, Beijing Opera, VGGSound, FSD50K, and AudioSet. The model's text output will be compared to label names using text embeddings and cosine similarity. Performance will be benchmarked against the contrastive audio-text model CLAP and a concurrent work.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to generate descriptive captions for audio clips on standard captioning benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The LTU model will be prompted to 'write an audio caption describing the sound'. The generated captions will be evaluated on the test sets of the Clotho v2 and AudioCaps benchmarks. Performance will be compared with SOTA specialized models.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's emerging audio reasoning and comprehension capabilities on open-ended tasks through qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
experiments.Method: A two-stage human subjective evaluation will be conducted via Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT). In Stage 1, human evaluators will assess LTU's answers to open-ended questions generated by GPT-4. In Stage 2, evaluators will assess LTU's answers to human-generated questions. Additionally, an automated evaluation using GPT-4 will be performed to assess the instruction-following rate of the model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Instruction following rate', 'Factual correctness rate', 'Human preference rate']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to justify the design choices of the training framework, including the use of LoRA adapters and the perception-to-understanding curriculum.
experiments.Method: Several versions of the LTU model will be trained and evaluated: 1) with full finetuning of LLaMA instead of LoRA, 2) without the perception-to-understanding curriculum, 3) trained only on closed-ended tasks, 4) without LoRA adapters, and 5) with a frozen audio branch. These models will be evaluated on classification and language tasks.

risk_factors: In this work, we mainly focus on general audio understanding and LTU thus has limited ability to understand speech content, i.e., it is not an automatic speech recognition model.

First, the LLaMA-7B model used in LTU is the smallest model in the LLaMA model family, LLaMA-13B, and larger models are more commonly used and may have a stronger reasoning and understanding ability. Particularly, larger LLM may exhibit emerging properties that the smaller LLM model does not have. While we show LTU exhibits stronger audio reasoning and understanding abilities than existing models, it could have been even stronger if a larger LLM was used.

Second, for the same computational efficiency consideration, we applied a 2× temporal downsampling to make the audio token frequency at 3.2Hz. This may limit the fine-grained temporal reasoning ability of the model.

Third, while we used 8 audio datasets to construct the OpenAQA-5M dataset, only a small portion of them are with temporal-level annotation (i.e., the onset and offset of the sound event), which is important information for GPT-3.5-Turbo to generate time-related questions. Therefore, the capability of the model to temporally localize the sound event might be weaker than its capability to recognize the sound events.

Finally, while we find closed-ended training and the unanswerable training questions greatly mitigate the hallucination issue (the model will answer "I do not know" to unanswerable questions), hallucination and bias may not be completely eliminated and may occur in some cases.

title: Observe-Then-Think: Learning to Elicit Multimodal Understanding by Decoupling Perception and Reasoning
name: Observe-Then-Think
short_hypothesis: Explicitly decoupling perception and reasoning into a structured 'observe-then-think' process, implemented via a two-stage post-training framework (SFT and RL), will elicit stronger perceptual abilities and improve multimodal reasoning in Vision-Language Models. The SFT stage will teach the model a structured output format, and the RL stage, using a Perception-Guided Consistency Optimization (PGCO) algorithm with specialized rewards, will jointly incentivize perceptual accuracy and reasoning consistency.
related_work: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown impressive capabilities but still struggle with complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. This is partly due to an imbalance between perceptual alignment and reasoning depth. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training, particularly methods like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has improved text-based reasoning, its application to VLMs is hindered because limited perception remains a key bottleneck. The central research question is whether RL can be used not only to sharpen reasoning but also to elicit stronger perceptual abilities in VLMs, thereby enabling more comprehensive multimodal reasoning. This work proposes to bridge this perception-reasoning gap by explicitly separating the two processes, inspired by the division of labor in the human brain's visual and prefrontal cortices.
abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved impressive results in fusing visual and textual inputs, yet they often stumble on tasks demanding complex, multimodal reasoning. This imbalance arises from the inherent separation between perception—accurately interpreting sensory data, and reasoning—conducting multi-step, symbolic inference. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel framework for multimodal reasoning, OTT (Observe-Then-Think), which includes a two-stage post-training process: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL). During SFT, the model learns to decouple perceptual understanding from logical inference, mastering structured output formats and ensuring logical consistency. In the RL stage, our Perception-Guided Consistency Optimization (PGCO) algorithm, inspired by human cognition, enhances visual understanding through perception rewards and employs consistency rewards to align perception and reasoning steps, ensuring the final answer accuracy, eliminating logical contradictions without external tool support.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall effectiveness and generality of the proposed OTT method against state-of-the-art closed and open-source models across a wide range of visual reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed OTT-7B model, based on Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct, will be evaluated against baselines including GPT-4o, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, InternVL2-8B, MiniCPM-V2.6-8B, Mulberry-7B, R1-VL-7B, and the base Qwen2-VL-7B model. The evaluation will be conducted on six benchmarks: MathVista, WeMath, BLINK, V*Bench, VisuLogic, and MME.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Benchmark-specific accuracy scores.', 'For MathVista and WeMath, a powerful judge model (Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct) will be used for evaluation.', 'For MME, a sum score will be used.']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the importance of the 'Observe' component within the reasoning framework.
experiments.Method: The performance of a base model (Qwen2-VL-7B) will be compared with a vanilla GRPO implementation and a GRPO implementation that incorporates the 'Observe' strategy. The evaluation will be performed on the MathVista and MME datasets.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed two-stage OTT method against alternative training strategies, specifically vanilla GRPO and SFT alone.
experiments.Method: The base model (Qwen2-VL-7B) will be trained using three different strategies: vanilla GRPO, SFT only, and the full OTT method. The comparison will be made on the V*Bench and VisuLogic datasets.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the synergistic effects of the perception and consistency reward components within the PGCO algorithm.
experiments.Method: The OTT framework will be evaluated with different reward configurations: using only the perception reward, using only the consistency reward, and using both rewards combined. The evaluation will be performed on the WeMath and MathVista datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of the OTT framework across different model sizes.
experiments.Method: The OTT framework will be applied to two base models of different scales, Qwen2-VL-2B and Qwen2-VL-7B. Their performance will be compared against their respective base models on the BLINK and MathVista benchmarks. This will be tested using both a 20k and a 260k SFT dataset.

experiments.Description: To assess the impact of varying SFT and RL data scales on the performance of the OTT framework.
experiments.Method: Using Qwen2-VL-7B as the base model, experiments will be run by: 1) varying the SFT data size (20k vs. 260k) while keeping the RL data size fixed, and 2) varying the RL data size (5k vs. 20k) while keeping the SFT data size fixed. The evaluation will be performed on the BLINK and MME datasets.

risk_factors: Although vision-language models (VLMs) have made great strides in combining visual and linguistic data, they still struggle with complex reasoning tasks requiring deep abstract understanding.

The main challenge is integrating perception, which handles sensory inputs, with reasoning, which involves symbolic and multi-step inference, and the lack of high-quality multimodal reasoning datasets further limits their real-world performance in areas like scientific analysis.
However, it also raises concerns about potential misuse, such as the creation of misleading content, necessitating careful and responsible deployment.

title: M$^3$E: Continual Vision-and-Language Navigation via Mixture of Macro and Micro Experts
name: m3e-continual-vln
short_hypothesis: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in continual Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) hinges on decoupling global scene reasoning from local perceptual alignment. A hierarchical Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with a dual-router architecture can achieve this by separating navigation into two levels of reasoning: a macro-level router for strategy based on global environment features and a micro-level router for perception based on local instruction-vision alignment. This allows an agent to adapt to new domains while preserving specialized capabilities, without needing to replay past data.
related_work: Most Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents are trained on static datasets and exhibit poor generalization and catastrophic forgetting when adapted to new environments. Existing continual learning (CL) methods for embodied agents primarily rely on rehearsal buffers, which replay past trajectories to mitigate forgetting. This approach increases storage and computational overhead and can raise privacy concerns. While replay-free MoE frameworks exist for other tasks like VQA, they have not been extended to VLN, which presents unique challenges like sequential planning under partial observability. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by introducing a replay-free, MoE-based framework specifically designed for continual VLN that separates global scene reasoning from local token-level grounding.
abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) agents have shown strong capabilities in following natural language instructions. However, they often struggle to generalize across environments due to catastrophic forgetting, which limits their practical use in real-world settings where agents must continually adapt to new domains. We argue that overcoming forgetting across environments hinges on decoupling global scene reasoning from local perceptual alignment, allowing the agent to adapt to new domains while preserving specialized capabilities. To this end, we propose M³E, the Mixture of Macro and Micro Experts, an environment-aware hierarchical MoE framework for continual VLN. Our method introduces a dual-router architecture that separates navigation into two levels of reasoning. A macro-level, scene-aware router selects strategy experts based on global environmental features (e.g., office vs. residential), while a micro-level, instance-aware router activates perception experts based on local instruction-vision alignment for step-wise decision making. To preserve knowledge across domains, we adopt a dynamic momentum update strategy that identifies expert utility in new environments and selectively updates or freezes their parameters.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed M³E method in a domain-incremental setting on the R2R dataset, assessing its ability to adapt to new scenes and retain knowledge of prior ones without revisiting past data.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the R2R dataset under a scene-partitioned continual stream protocol. A base agent is pretrained on the 'train' split. The 'val-unseen' scenes are partitioned into disjoint domains, each defining a continual learning task for sequential fine-tuning. The M³E model, built on a NaviLLM backbone with MoE-LORA, will be compared against several baselines: naive Finetune, regularization-based methods (L2, EWC), and rehearsal-based methods (ER, PerR, ESR, Dual-SR).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate (SR)', 'Success weighted by Path Length (SPL)', 'Navigation Error (NE)', 'Oracle Success Rate (OSR)', 'Backward Transfer (BWT)', 'Forward Transfer (FWT)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the M³E method on the more challenging, goal-oriented REVERIE dataset using the same domain-incremental protocol to test its effectiveness on object-centric navigation tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the REVERIE dataset under a scene-partitioned continual stream protocol. The M³E model will be compared against a naive Finetune baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate (SR)', 'Success weighted by Path Length (SPL)', 'Backward Transfer (BWT)', 'Forward Transfer (FWT)']

experiments.Description: To probe how much knowledge about previously seen environments is forgotten and how overall generalization changes when adapting to new domains in a bulk training setting, rather than task-by-task.
experiments.Method: Officially released checkpoints for HAMT, NaviLLM, and the proposed M³E model will be loaded and then training will be continued on the entire 'val-unseen' split of the R2R and REVERIE datasets. Performance will be evaluated on the 'val-seen' and 'test-unseen' splits both before and after this bulk training.

risk_factors: First, while our experiments are conducted in discrete simulators to adhere to standard VLN protocols, the proposed macro-micro decomposition is inherently compatible with continuous control.

Bridging the Sim-to-Real gap requires further addressing challenges such as visual domain gaps (e.g., lighting, motion blur) and real-world SLAM estimation errors, though our robustness analysis (Appendix A.3) suggests resilience to map noise.
Second, the fixed expert configuration may limit flexibility under extreme open-ended scenarios.
Future work includes dynamic expert allocation, privacy-aware hybrid replay, Sim-to-Real transfer and extension to physical robotic platforms.

title: UrbanPlanBench: A Comprehensive Assessment of Urban Planning Abilities in Large Language Models
name: urbanplanbench-eval
short_hypothesis: A comprehensive benchmark, UrbanPlanBench, based on real-world urban planner qualification exams, can quantitatively assess the proficiency of LLMs in urban planning. Furthermore, a large-scale supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset, UrbanPlanText, created from textbooks and past exams, can be used to enhance the domain-specific knowledge and reasoning abilities of these models.
related_work: While general-purpose benchmarks like SuperGLUE, BIG-BENCH, and C-Eval exist, along with domain-specific ones like LawBench and BizBench, there is a notable absence of dedicated benchmarks for urban planning. This gap restricts the ability to quantify the extent to which LLMs acquire specialized knowledge in this field. Similarly, there are no off-the-shelf resources for fine-tuning LLMs specifically for urban planning, creating a need for both a tailored evaluation suite and a domain-specific fine-tuning dataset.
abstract: Urban planning is a professional discipline that shapes our daily surroundings, which demands multifaceted domain knowledge and relies heavily on human expertise. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) holds promise for revolutionizing such a field by leveraging their pre-trained world knowledge. However, the extent to which these models can assist human practitioners remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we introduce a comprehensive benchmark, UrbanPlanBench, tailored to evaluate the efficacy of LLMs in urban planning, which encompasses fundamental principles, professional knowledge, and management and regulations, aligning closely with the qualifications expected of human planners. Besides the benchmark, we present the largest-ever supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset, UrbanPlanText, for LLMs in urban planning, comprising over 30,000 instruction pairs sourced from urban planning exams and textbooks. The proposed benchmark, dataset, and associated evaluation and fine-tuning toolsets aim to catalyze the integration of LLMs into practical urban computing.

experiments.Description: To assess the acquisition of urban planning skills by a diverse array of advanced LLMs using a new comprehensive benchmark, and to compare their performance against a human professional standard.
experiments.Method: A diverse set of open-source and commercial LLMs (including LLaMA series, Gemma series, ChatGLM series, Baichuan2, Qwen series, Yi, and ChatGPT 3.5/4o) will be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench. The benchmark consists of multiple-choice questions (both single-correct and multiple-correct) across three subjects: S1 (Fundamental principles), S2 (Professional knowledge), and S3 (Management and regulations). Performance will be compared against random guessing and the certification bar for human planners.

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of model size on performance on the UrbanPlanBench benchmark, examining potential scaling laws.
experiments.Method: Qwen1.5 models of varying parameter scales (ranging from 0.8B to 32B) will be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench. Their performance on both single-correct (MCQ-S) and multiple-correct (MCQ-M) questions will be analyzed in relation to model size.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of prompting techniques, specifically Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT), for enhancing LLM competence in urban planning tasks.
experiments.Method: Using GPT-4o-mini as the base model, its performance on UrbanPlanBench will be evaluated with and without prompting techniques. RAG experiments will use a knowledge base of urban planning textbooks and past exams. CoT experiments will include both few-shot and zero-shot variants.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a domain-specific dataset on the urban planning capabilities of LLMs.
experiments.Method: Several LLMs (LLaMA3, ChatGLM3, Baichuan2, Qwen1.5, Yi) will be fine-tuned on the newly created UrbanPlanText dataset using the LoRA method. The performance of these fine-tuned models will then be evaluated on the UrbanPlanBench and compared against their pre-SFT performance.

risk_factors: Moving forward, our future work includes expanding both UrbanPlanBench and UrbanPlanText to incorporate multi-linguistic urban planning materials, thereby enabling broader use cases of the benchmark and dataset.

Additionally, we aim to extend UrbanPlanBench into a multi-modal benchmark, integrating both imagery of urban plans and their corresponding descriptive text, further enriching the evaluation capabilities of LLMs in urban planning contexts.

title: Permutation Equivariant Neural Networks for Antisymmetric Tensors
name: Permutation-Equivariant-Nets-for-Antisymmetric-Tensors
short_hypothesis: A neural network architecture built upon a complete characterisation of all linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric power spaces of R^n, implemented via a memory-efficient approach termed 'IO patterns', will outperform standard MLPs and general permutation equivariant networks on tasks where the target function depends on the antisymmetric structure of the input.
related_work: Existing permutation equivariant architectures are designed for general or symmetric tensors and do not guarantee the preservation of the antisymmetric structure, limiting their effectiveness on such data. While some work has explored SO(3) equivariance for antisymmetric tensors, permutation equivariance remains largely unexplored. This work aims to address this gap by providing a complete characterisation of permutation equivariant linear maps for antisymmetric tensors.
abstract: Antisymmetric tensors, which change sign under index swaps, appear naturally in physics, yet learning from them remains largely unexplored. We provide a complete characterisation of all linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric power spaces of R^n. To make this characterisation practical, we introduce a memory-efficient implementation that eliminates the need to create and store large weight matrices. Our approach is intended to be efficient in learning functions that depend on the antisymmetric structure of the input and to incorporate this structure as an inductive bias.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model on a synthetic S8-equivariant task involving mapping antisymmetric tensors to vectors, where the target function depends on the input's antisymmetric structure.
experiments.Method: A synthetic dataset of 10,000 training and 2,000 test antisymmetric tensors in (R^8)^2 is generated. The task is to learn the function f(T)_i = σ(Σ_{j:j≠i} T_{i,j}). The proposed model, 'AntiSymmPermEquiv', is constructed with S8-equivariant linear layers designed for antisymmetric tensors. It will be compared against two baselines: 'PermEquiv', a standard S8-equivariant model for general tensors, and a standard 'MLP'.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model on an S4-invariant task of learning the absolute value of the Pfaffian of 4x4 antisymmetric matrices.
experiments.Method: Synthetic datasets of 30, 300, 3000, and 30000 antisymmetric tensors in (R^4)^2 are generated for training, with a test set of 1000 tensors. The proposed model, 'AntiSymmPermEquiv', is constructed with S4-equivariant and S4-invariant layers for antisymmetric tensors. It will be compared against two baselines: 'PermEquiv', a standard S4-invariant model for general tensors, and a standard 'MLP'.

risk_factors: This work is primarily theoretical, providing a complete characterisation of linear permutation equivariant functions between antisymmetric tensors. While its immediate practical impact may be limited, we believe that it lays a principled foundation for future developments in neural network architectures and learning algorithms that are capable of operating on data that can be represented by antisymmetric tensors, with potential applications in real-world domains where these structures naturally arise.

title: SIGMA-Gen: Structure and Identity Guided Multi-Subject Assembly for Image Generation
name: sigma-gen
short_hypothesis: A single diffusion transformer model, trained on a large-scale synthetic dataset, can perform single-pass image generation of multiple subjects while preserving their identities and adhering to diverse spatial and structural constraints. These constraints can range from coarse 2D/3D bounding boxes to fine-grained pixel-level masks and depth maps. This is achieved by encoding identity, routing, and structure information into dedicated control images that are processed through a unified attention mechanism.
related_work: Prior work in controllable image generation falls into several categories. Structure control methods like ControlNet and T2I-Adapter can guide generation using depth or segmentation but lack mechanisms for controlling subject identity. Subject personalization methods like DreamBooth and Textual Inversion can learn a subject's identity but typically require per-subject optimization and struggle to compose multiple subjects with spatial control. In-context generation methods can use reference images but also face challenges with multiple subjects and lack structural guidance. Subject insertion methods like AnyDoor are often limited to a single subject per generation. A key gap is the lack of a unified framework that can jointly handle multi-subject identity preservation and multi-granularity structural control within a single forward pass, which is largely due to the scarcity of suitable training data.
abstract: We present SIGMA-GEN, a unified framework for multi-identity preserving image generation. Unlike prior approaches, SIGMA-GEN is the first to enable single-pass multi-subject identity-preserved generation guided by both structural and spatial constraints. A key strength of our method is its ability to support user guidance at various levels of precision — from coarse 2D or 3D boxes to pixel-level segmentations and depth — with a single model. To enable this, we introduce SIGMA-SET27K, a novel synthetic dataset that provides identity, structure, and spatial information for over 100k unique subjects across 27k images.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SIGMA-GEN on single- and multi-subject personalized generation against baselines when provided with precise control signals (masks and depth).
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on a custom evaluation dataset of 710 examples. SIGMA-GEN, based on the Flux.1 Kontext model, will be compared against several baselines. For single-subject generation, baselines will include UniCombine, OminiControl, a variant of Insert Anything (Insert Anything*), and a variant of Flux Kontext (Flux Kontext*). For multi-subject generation, the baseline will be an iterative version of Insert Anything*.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['DINO-I (image-to-image similarity for identity)', 'SigLIP-I (image-to-image similarity for identity)', 'SigLIP-T (text-to-image similarity)', 'MSE (mean squared error for depth)', 'CLIP-IQA (perceptual quality)', 'MUSIQ (perceptual quality)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SIGMA-GEN on single- and multi-subject personalized generation against baselines when provided with coarse control signals (2D bounding boxes and 3D bounding box masks).
experiments.Method: Using the same custom evaluation dataset and the single trained SIGMA-GEN model, performance will be compared against the MSDiffusion baseline for tasks involving 2D bounding box control.

experiments.Description: To analyze the scalability of SIGMA-GEN in terms of performance and runtime as the number of subjects increases, and compare it to an iterative baseline approach.
experiments.Method: The performance of SIGMA-GEN will be measured on generations with an increasing number of subjects (from 2 to 7). This will be compared against the Iterative Insert Anything* baseline under the same conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the impact of different guidance components on the model's performance in the precise mask and depth scenario.
experiments.Method: The performance of the model will be tested under several conditions: 1) removing depth control entirely while keeping masks, 2) providing a full scene description in the text prompt versus a background-only prompt. A further ablation will probe different methods of providing depth control: via the unified structure control image (subject-only or full depth) versus using an external pre-trained ControlNet.

risk_factors: In terms of subject consistency, measured by DINO-I and SigLIP-I, SIGMA-GEN experiences some degradation as the number of subjects increases—largely due to dataset distribution effects.

title: NeoBabel: An Inclusive Multilingual Open Tower for Visual Generation
name: multilingual-t2i-generation-neobabel
short_hypothesis: A novel multilingual image generation framework, NEOBABEL, can be developed to provide a scalable solution for direct text-to-image synthesis across multiple languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian) without requiring intermediate translation. By building upon a pretrained multilingual large language model and introducing a unified multimodal embedding space and modality-aware attention, the model is intended to achieve language-agnostic understanding, maintaining performance parity with or exceeding English-only models while being more efficient.
related_work: Existing text-to-image generation systems are predominantly English-centric, creating barriers for non-English speakers. The common workaround is a 'translation-first' strategy, where non-English prompts are translated to English before generation. This approach introduces significant problems, including computational overhead which increases inference time, and semantic drift, where culturally specific meanings and linguistic subtleties are lost in translation. This results in generated images that fail to capture the user's intended meaning. There is a need for a direct, native multilingual text-to-image generation model that avoids these pitfalls.
abstract: Text-to-image generation advancements have been predominantly English-centric, creating barriers for non-English speakers and perpetuating digital inequities. While existing systems rely on translation pipelines, these introduce semantic drift, computational overhead, and cultural misalignment. We introduce NEOBABEL, a novel multilingual image generation framework that sets a new Pareto frontier in performance, efficiency and inclusivity, supporting six languages: English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, and Persian. The model is trained using a combination of large-scale multilingual pretraining and high-resolution instruction tuning. To evaluate its capabilities, we plan to expand two English-only benchmarks to multilingual equivalents: m-GenEval and m-DPG.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's multilingual image generation performance on compositional prompts against both generative and unified models.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the m-GenEval benchmark, a multilingual extension of GenEval, across six languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian). The task involves assessing prompt-to-image alignment across six compositional dimensions: single object, two objects, counting, colors, position, and color attribute. Baselines for comparison are intended to include generative models (LlamaGen, PixArt-alpha, SDXL, SD3) and unified models (Chameleon, LWM, TokenFlow, EMU3, Show-o, Janus-Pro, BLIP3-o).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on general-purpose multilingual generation with open-ended, diverse prompts that test broader semantic understanding.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the m-DPG benchmark, a multilingual extension of DPG-Bench, across six languages (English, Chinese, Dutch, French, Hindi, Persian). Baselines for comparison are intended to include Show-o, Janus Pro, and BLIP3-o.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively assess the model's ability to generate semantically aligned and visually coherent images from multilingual prompts across diverse categories.
experiments.Method: The model will be used to generate images from prompts in six languages covering diverse categories, including compositional scenes, abstract concepts, and multilingual instructions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection for semantic alignment, visual coherence, and consistency across languages']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's zero-shot capability for text-guided image inpainting and extrapolation using multilingual prompts.
experiments.Method: The model will be tasked with modifying or extending input images based on prompts provided in various languages, without any task-specific fine-tuning.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection for semantic faithfulness and visual consistency with adjacent content']

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of the progressive pretraining and instruction tuning strategies on the model's multilingual performance.
experiments.Method: Model checkpoints from different training stages (Pixel Dependency Learning, Scaling Alignment, Refined Pretraining, Initial Instruction Alignment, Instruction Refinement) will be evaluated on the m-GenEval and m-DPG benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's robustness to code-switching, where prompts combine multiple languages within the same input.
experiments.Method: The model will be tested on specially designed cross-lingual prompts created by splitting a base prompt and translating segments into different languages. The evaluation will use prompts from the m-DPG benchmark.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Code-Switching Similarity (CSS) score, using EVA-CLIP and DINOv2 embeddings']

risk_factors: First, the model currently supports six languages; extending to broader linguistic coverage would require tokenizer adaptation and additional training.
Second, although NEOBABEL adopts a unified architecture, it does not yet support vision-language tasks such as visual question answering, due to the absence of task-specific fine-tuning.

title: When Reasoning Meets Compression: Understanding the Effects of LLMs Compression on Large Reasoning Models
name: lrm-compression-effects-and-interpretation
short_hypothesis: The importance shift of weights should be minimal in an ideal compression scenario, as a compressed Large Reasoning Model (LRM) should remain as close as possible to its original, more reasoning-capable counterpart. Analyzing the change in relative importance of weights after compression can identify which reasoning capabilities are diminished and locate critical weights for reasoning.
related_work: Existing studies on compressing Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) are insufficient. They either fail to comprehensively compare the three main compression methods (quantization, distillation, pruning) or lack in-depth interpretation of how compression affects model internals. While some works benchmark quantization or pruning, they often use general-purpose LLM benchmarks rather than reasoning-intensive tasks. Distillation studies often do not evaluate on diverse reasoning tasks or consider the effect on parametric knowledge. A significant research gap is the lack of interpretability, which is necessary to understand how compression methods affect LRMs, reveal bottlenecks, and guide future research.
abstract: Compression methods, including quantization, distillation, and pruning, improve the computational efficiency of large reasoning models (LRMs). However, existing studies either fail to sufficiently compare all three compression methods on LRMs or lack in-depth interpretation analysis. In this paper, we investigate how the reasoning capabilities of LRMs are compromised during compression, through performance benchmarking and mechanistic interpretation. To uncover the effects of compression on reasoning performance, we propose to benchmark quantized, distilled, and pruned DeepSeek-R1 models on four reasoning datasets (AIME 2024, FOLIO, Temporal Sequences, and MuSiQue). To precisely locate compression effects on model weights, we plan to adapt difference of means and attribution patching techniques, focusing on the activation of every linear component in compressed LRMs, to interpret fine-grained causal relationships between weights and various reasoning capabilities. This fine-grained interpretation is intended to address a fundamental question of compression: which weights are the most important for reasoning?

experiments.Description: To benchmark the performance of various compressed versions of the DeepSeek-R1 model against the original model to understand how different compression strategies affect reasoning capabilities.
experiments.Method: The DeepSeek-R1 model and its distilled variants (R1-Distill-Llama, R1-Distill-Qwen) will be compressed using multiple techniques. These include dynamic quantization (Unsloth at 2.51, 1.73, 1.58-bit), post-training quantization (AWQ, GPTQ, GPTAQ, ANY4/3 at 4-bit and 3-bit), and pruning (SparseGPT, AlphaPruning at various sparsities). These models will be evaluated on a suite of reasoning tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on AIME 2024, FOLIO, and Temporal Sequences.', 'Exact Match (EM) and F1 score on MuSiQue.']

experiments.Description: To mechanistically interpret how compression affects model weights by quantifying the contribution of each linear module to specific reasoning capabilities and tracking how this importance shifts after compression.
experiments.Method: Using the original and compressed LRMs, we will adapt 'difference of means' and 'attribution patching' techniques. This will be used to compute an importance score for each linear module with respect to four core reasoning behaviors: backtracking, uncertainty estimation, example testing, and adding knowledge. We will then calculate the 'importance shift' (change in relative importance) from the original model to its compressed variant.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Importance scores (I_ml) for each module.', 'Relative importance shift (change of RI_ml) visualized via heatmaps.']

experiments.Description: To validate the importance scores by selectively quantizing components identified as most important and measuring the resulting performance degradation.
experiments.Method: For a distilled LRM (R1 distilled Llama-8B), we will apply 3-bit round-to-nearest quantization to a single component at a time. The components chosen will be those identified as most important (e.g., the up_projection in the final layer) and other components for comparison (e.g., other modules in the same layer or other layers in the same module type).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy drop on AIME 2024, FOLIO, and Temporal Sequences benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that current quantization methods overly compress certain modules by designing a selective protection mechanism and evaluating its effect on performance.
experiments.Method: We will apply 3-bit AWQ quantization to an LRM. In a separate run, we will apply the same quantization but protect the MLP modules in the final layer by keeping them at their original 16-bit precision. The performance of this mixed-precision model will be compared to the standard 3-bit quantized model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, EM, and F1 scores on the AIME 2024, FOLIO, Temporal Sequences, and MuSiQue benchmarks.']

risk_factors: We focus on analyzing the effect of compression methods on performance and thus do not consider inference speedup. The reason is that these methods run on different inference platforms, so it is hard to control the consistency of inference optimization across various platforms.

title: Learning from One and Only One Shot
name: learning-topological-similarity-via-distortable-canvas
short_hypothesis: By computationally modeling innate human priors about object distortion via a 'distortable canvas', it is possible to learn a transformation-based topological similarity metric. This metric, when used with a simple classifier like nearest-neighbor, will enable learning from very few examples (1-10) without any pre-training, unlike standard few-shot learning methods.
related_work: Modern machine learning models are data-hungry and complex. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses data scarcity but relies on pre-training on large, relevant source datasets, which can be difficult to select and may lead to negative transfer. Other approaches build transformations into the model (e.g., optimal-transport maps, data augmentation), but these often encode specific domain knowledge (like rotation or scaling) rather than considering all possible transformations. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by creating a model that learns a general, transformation-based similarity from first principles, requiring no pre-training at all.
abstract: Humans can generalize from only a few examples and from little pre-training on similar tasks. Yet, machine learning (ML) typically requires large data to learn or pre-learn to transfer. Inspired by nativism, we directly model basic human-innate priors in abstract visual tasks e.g., character/doodle recognition. This yields a white-box model that learns transformation-based topological similarity by mimicking how humans naturally “distort" an object when first seeing it. Using the simple nearest-neighbor classifier in this similarity space, our model is designed for character recognition using only 1–10 examples per class and no pre-training. This differs from few-shot learning (FSL) using significant pre-training. Further, by mimicking k-means but in a non-Euclidean space, our model enables unsupervised learning and can generate human-interpretable archetypes as cluster “centroids".

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's classification performance in a 'tiny-data' regime on the MNIST dataset, training on only the first N images per class.
experiments.Method: The learned Dc- / Dv-distance is used with a nearest-neighbor classifier (Dc- / Dv-nearest-neighbor). The model is trained on the first N images per class from the MNIST training set, where N ranges from 1 to 20. Baselines for comparison include neural-network-based models (TextCaps, CNN+dropout, Capsule+Siamese) and classical ML models (SVM, Nearest-Neighbor, Decision Tree, Random Forest).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test set accuracy versus the number of training examples per class (N)', 'The minimum number of training examples per class needed to reach a 90% accuracy threshold']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's classification performance in a 'tiny-data' regime on the more challenging EMNIST-letters dataset.
experiments.Method: The learned Dc- / Dv-distance is used with a nearest-neighbor classifier. The model is trained on the first N images per class from the EMNIST-letters training set, where N ranges from 1 to 20. Baselines for comparison include TextCaps, SVM, Nearest-Neighbor, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and a standard Neural Network.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test set accuracy versus the number of training examples per class (N)', 'The minimum number of training examples per class needed to reach a 75% accuracy threshold']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model on the Omniglot one-shot classification challenge, which involves learning from a single example per class without pre-training.
experiments.Method: The Dc-nearest-neighbor model is run on the Omniglot one-shot task. It uses one training image and one test image per character in each 20-way classification task. The model is run without using any background set for pre-training or any stroke-movement information. Baselines include BPL, Humans, RCN, Siamese ConvNet, Simple ConvNet, Prototypical Net, and VHE.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Error rate on the 20-way one-shot classification tasks']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the model's ability to perform unsupervised clustering and generate human-interpretable archetypes for a given class of images.
experiments.Method: A k-means-style clustering algorithm is adapted to use the learned topological similarity space. This is applied to a set of images of the digit '7' from MNIST and a set of 'giraffe' doodles from the Quick, Draw! dataset. The elbow method will be used to select a suitable number of clusters (k).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Within-cluster sum of distances (WCSD) versus the number of clusters (k)', 'Qualitative inspection of the generated cluster centroids (archetypes) for interpretability']

risk_factors: This paper focuses on the first type, handling abstract images only, by modeling humans’ distortion-based intuition.

Although our model has shown dominant classification performance in the tiny-data regime of the presented benchmarks, its dominance diminishes when training size increases. This is due to 1-NN being 100% biased towards the “nearest neighbor” and hence fragile against noisy, erroneous, and ambiguous training examples.
Being sensitive to ambiguities and outliers, however, is not a deficiency of our distortable canvas model, but a property of nearest-neighbor.
A related issue due to lacking validation data is about picking a proper model configuration.
By contrast, our distortable canvas model requires little to tune, other than the (G, pc)-solution path.

title: Exploring Redundancy and Shared Representations for Transformer Models Optimization
name: transformer-redundancy-and-compression-strategies
short_hypothesis: Structural and weight redundancies in Transformer architectures can be exploited for model compression through three novel techniques: 1) MASS, which enforces weight aggregation and sharing across blocks; 2) GlobaL Fact, which uses a shared global low-rank factor and layer-specific local factors; and 3) ABACO, which progressively transfers knowledge to low-rank adapters.
related_work: Prior work in model compression includes pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and low-rank factorization. Simple weight-sharing strategies have often failed to preserve accuracy, suggesting that redundancy is more subtle than a simple duplication of parameters. The degree of functional overlap across layers in Transformers, and its implications for structured compression, remains insufficiently understood.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) deliver state-of-the-art performance but at the cost of extreme computational and energy demands, raising the question of how much of their capacity is truly necessary. This paper explores structural and weight redundancies in Transformer-based architectures, aiming to identify inefficiencies and leverage them through targeted compression techniques. A central focus is assessing whether different modules perform overlapping functions and whether model matrices exhibit an inherently low-rank structure. To further explore these aspects, three novel compression methods are introduced: MASS, which enforces weight aggregation and sharing, along with two factorization-based techniques, GlobaL Fact and ABACO. This paper aims to contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the efficiency of LLMs.

experiments.Description: To investigate the degree of functional redundancy in Transformer models by assessing the performance impact of swapping modules between different layers.
experiments.Method: For Llama 3.1 (8B) and Gemma 2 (2B) models, systematically exchange feed-forward network (FFNN) and self-attention modules between different Transformer blocks (e.g., replace the module from block `i` with the one from block `i+k`). The performance of the modified model will be evaluated against a baseline where the original module is ablated instead of replaced. The evaluation will be conducted on language modeling benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Matrix Aggregation and Sharing Strategy (MASS) for model compression.
experiments.Method: Apply MASS to Llama 3.1 (8B). Layers will be partitioned into groups of structurally compatible matrices (e.g., all 'query' matrices). Within each group, weights and biases will be merged into a single shared layer via simple averaging. The compressed model will then be fine-tuned on the OpenWebText dataset. The evaluation will compare the performance of the compressed model against the original, uncompressed model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of parameters / Compression Ratio', 'Performance on HellaSwag (before and after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (before and after fine-tuning)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the Global and Local Factorization (GlobaL Fact) compression method, which factorizes weights into a shared global matrix and layer-specific local matrices.
experiments.Method: Apply GlobaL Fact to the weight matrices of Llama 3.1 (8B). Selected weight matrices will be approximated as the product of a shared global matrix (Gs) and a layer-specific local matrix (Lj). The global matrix will be initialized using SVD. The compressed model will be fine-tuned on OpenWebText. The approach will be compared against a standard Truncated SVD baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of parameters / Compression Ratio', 'Performance on HellaSwag (before and after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (before and after fine-tuning)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the Adapter-Based Approximation and Compression Optimization (ABACO) method, which progressively transfers knowledge from full-rank matrices to low-rank adapters.
experiments.Method: Apply ABACO to Gemma 2 (2B). The method integrates LoRA adapters while fine-tuning on OpenWebText. During training, the contribution of the original pre-trained weights is progressively reduced via an exponential decay schedule, transferring knowledge to the trainable low-rank adapters. The original weights are later discarded or approximated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance on HellaSwag (after fine-tuning)', 'Performance on GSM8K (after fine-tuning)', 'Training and validation loss curves']

risk_factors: simple weight-sharing strategies often fail to preserve accuracy, indicating that redundancy may be more subtle than anticipated.
replacing multiple layers with a shared representation inevitably reduces layer-specific expressivity.
Exploring more sophisticated grouping criteria is left for future work.
Alternative strategies (e.g., weighted averaging or clustering-based aggregation) may better capture shared features and are left for future research

title: Streaming Visual Geometry Transformer
name: streaming-visual-geometry-transformer
short_hypothesis: A causal transformer architecture using temporal causal attention and cached historical keys/values can perform streaming, low-latency 3D reconstruction from video by incrementally integrating information while maintaining high-quality spatial consistency.
related_work: Perceiving and reconstructing 3D geometry from video is a fundamental computer vision task. However, existing approaches like the dense bidirectional visual geometry grounded transformer (VGGT) are not designed for interactive and low-latency applications, creating a need for an efficient, online, streaming model.
abstract: Perceiving and reconstructing 3D geometry from videos is a fundamental yet challenging computer vision task. To facilitate interactive and low-latency applications, we propose a streaming visual geometry transformer that shares a similar philosophy with autoregressive large language models. We explore a simple and efficient design and employ a causal transformer architecture to process the input sequence in an online manner. We use temporal causal attention and cache the historical keys and values as implicit memory to enable efficient streaming long-term 3D reconstruction. This design is intended to handle low-latency 3D reconstruction by incrementally integrating historical information while maintaining high-quality spatial consistency. For efficient training, we propose to distill knowledge from the dense bidirectional visual geometry grounded transformer (VGGT) to our causal model. For inference, our model supports the migration of optimized efficient attention operators (e.g., FlashAttention) from large language models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if the proposed streaming model can enhance inference speed in online scenarios while maintaining competitive performance.
experiments.Method: The proposed streaming visual geometry transformer will be evaluated on various 3D geometry perception benchmarks. The model will be compared to other methods, including the dense bidirectional visual geometry grounded transformer (VGGT) which serves as a teacher model.

title: Patch-Fool: Are Vision Transformers Always Robust Against Adversarial Perturbations?
name: patch-fool-attack
short_hypothesis: Customized adversarial perturbations targeting a single basic component (a patch) of a Vision Transformer's self-attention mechanism can be more effective in fooling the model compared to standard attacks, potentially revealing that ViTs are not inherently more robust than CNNs.
related_work: Recent studies suggest that Vision Transformers (ViTs) are more robust against adversarial attacks than Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The prevailing conjecture is that ViTs' focus on global interactions among input patches improves their robustness to local perturbations. However, these evaluations primarily use existing attack methods designed for or biased towards CNNs. This raises the question of whether ViTs are truly robust against all types of perturbations or if their perceived robustness is merely an artifact of biased evaluation. The research gap lies in the lack of dedicated adversarial attacks that are customized to exploit the unique architectural properties of ViTs, such as their patch-based structure and self-attention mechanism.
abstract: Recent works show that Vision Transformers (ViTs) are more robust against adversarial attacks as compared with convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and conjecture that this is because ViTs focus more on capturing global interactions among different input/feature patches. In this work, we ask an intriguing question: "Under what kinds of perturbations do ViTs become more vulnerable learners compared to CNNs?" Driven by this question, we first plan to conduct a comprehensive experiment regarding the robustness of both ViTs and CNNs under various existing adversarial attacks to understand the underlying reason favoring their robustness. Based on the anticipated insights, we then propose a dedicated attack framework, dubbed Patch-Fool, that fools the self-attention mechanism by attacking its basic component (i.e., a single patch) with a series of attention-aware optimization techniques.

experiments.Description: To validate the common belief about ViT robustness, a preliminary experiment will evaluate several representative ViT and CNN models against existing state-of-the-art adversarial attacks.
experiments.Method:
Models: DeiT, Swin, LeViT, ResNet, and VGG families.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Attacks: PGD, AutoAttack, CW-L_inf, and CW-L2 with various perturbation strengths.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the effectiveness of the proposed Patch-Fool attack, its impact on the robustness of ViTs will be compared against that on CNNs.
experiments.Method:
Models: DeiT and ResNet families.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Attack: The proposed Patch-Fool attack, varying the number of perturbed patches from one to four. For CNNs, patch selection will be guided by a saliency map.

experiments.Description: To validate the design of the patch selection mechanism, an ablation study will be conducted to compare the proposed attention-aware method with other strategies.
experiments.Method:
Models: DeiT and ResNet families.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Attack: Patch-Fool with three patch selection strategies: (1) attention-aware (proposed), (2) random patch selection, and (3) saliency-map-based patch selection.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed attention-aware loss function, an ablation study will compare it with simpler loss formulations.
experiments.Method:
Model: DeiT-Base.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Method: The attack will be performed using three loss variants: (1) the full attention-aware loss with cosine-similarity-based re-weighting, (2) only the final cross-entropy loss, and (3) a direct summation of cross-entropy and layerwise attention-aware losses without re-weighting.

experiments.Description: To investigate the influence of perturbation density on model robustness, a sparse variant of the attack, Sparse Patch-Fool, will be evaluated.
experiments.Method:
Models: DeiT and ResNet families.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Attack: Sparse Patch-Fool, varying the global perturbation ratio (PR) and the number of patches allowed to be perturbed.

experiments.Description: To investigate the influence of perturbation strength within a patch, a mild variant of the attack, Mild Patch-Fool, will be evaluated under norm constraints.
experiments.Method:
Models: DeiT and ResNet families.
Dataset: ImageNet.
Attack: Mild Patch-Fool with L2 or L_inf constraints on the patch-wise perturbations, varying the perturbation strength epsilon.

risk_factors: enhanced adversarial training schemes or other defense methods are required to robustify ViTs against our Patch-Fool, which is also our future work.

title: On the Optimality of Activations in Implicit Neural Representations
name: sinc-activations-for-inrs
short_hypothesis: From the perspective of sampling theory, the sinc activation function is theoretically optimal for signal encoding in shallow Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). We propose that using sinc as an activation function will enable INRs to optimally reconstruct signals while preserving smooth gradients, outperforming other common activations like sinusoids, Gaussians, and wavelets.
related_work: Prior work on Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) has explored various techniques to capture high-frequency signals, addressing the spectral bias of standard networks. These include positional encodings, sinusoidal activations (SIREN), Gaussian activations (GARF), and wavelet-based activations (WIRE). However, these methods were developed and analyzed in isolation. This fragmented approach has made it difficult to draw connections, conduct effective comparisons, and establish a unified theoretical framework for understanding the properties and optimality of different activation functions in INRs.
abstract: Implicit neural representations (INRs) have recently surged in popularity as a class of neural networks capable of encoding signals as compact, differentiable entities. To capture high-frequency content, INRs often employ techniques such as Fourier positional encodings or non-traditional activation functions like Gaussian, sinusoid, or wavelets. Despite the impressive results achieved with these activations, there has been limited exploration of their properties within a unified theoretical framework. To address this gap, we propose to conduct a comprehensive analysis of these activations from the perspective of sampling theory. We hypothesize that, particularly in the context of shallow INRs, sinc activations are theoretically optimal for signal encoding. Additionally, we plan to establish a connection between dynamical systems and INRs and leverage sampling theory to bridge these two paradigms, exploring the application of INRs in modeling such systems with minimal need for explicit regularizations.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance and parameter robustness of different INR activations for image reconstruction.
experiments.Method: INRs with different activations (sinc, SIREN, ReLU with Positional Encoding, Gaussian, Wavelet) will be trained on a sub-sampled set of the DIV2K dataset. The models will be 4-layer networks with a width of 256. A grid search will be performed to find a single optimal hyperparameter setting for each activation type, which will then be used across all images and various sampling rates to train the models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of sinc-activated INRs for novel view synthesis in the context of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF).
experiments.Method: The performance of sinc-INRs will be evaluated on the novel view synthesis task using the real synthetic dataset from Mildenhall et al. (2021). The proposed method will be compared against INRs using other activations such as Gaussian, Sinusoid, and Wavelet.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of sinc-INRs to recover the dynamics of latent variables in chaotic dynamical systems from partial, noisy, and sparse observations.
experiments.Method: Using data from dynamical systems (Vanderpol, Limit cycle, Lorenz, Duffing), the experiment will compare two approaches for recovering the system's attractor. The baseline 'classical method' involves constructing a Hankel matrix from discrete samples and using its eigenvectors. The proposed method first uses a sinc-INR to encode the discrete measurements into a continuous signal, which is then re-sampled to create a more robust Hankel matrix. The methods' robustness will be tested by adding varying levels of noise to the input samples.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of using sinc-INRs to improve the discovery of governing equations for dynamical systems via the SINDy algorithm.
experiments.Method: Using data from the Lorenz and Rossler systems with varying levels of noise, the experiment will compare two methods for computing the time derivatives required by the SINDy algorithm. The baseline method will use spectral derivatives. The proposed method will first train a 4-layer sinc-INR on the time-series data and then compute the derivatives analytically by calculating the network's Jacobian. The resulting derivatives from both methods will be used in the SINDy algorithm to recover the system's governing equations.

risk_factors: The primary limitation of Theorem 2.4 lies in its applicability solely to signals within the domain of V(F).
A critical problem entailed with INRs is that they are sensitive to the hyperparameters in activation functions (Ramasinghe & Lucey, 2022).
The theorem does not provide conditions as to what the best n is and in practise when D is not known it does not provide conditions for the embedding dimension d.

title: SimpleVLA-RL: Scaling VLA Training via Reinforcement Learning
name: simplevla-rl
short_hypothesis: Inspired by LLM breakthroughs where RL with outcome rewards enhances step-by-step reasoning, it is hypothesized that an outcome-driven RL paradigm can improve the long-horizon, step-by-step action planning of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. This can be achieved with an efficient RL framework, SimpleVLA-RL, which builds on veRL and introduces VLA-specific trajectory sampling, scalable parallelization, multi-environment rendering, optimized loss computation, and exploration-enhancing strategies to scale VLA training beyond limited static datasets.
related_work: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, typically trained via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), are constrained by the scarcity and high cost of large-scale, high-quality robotic trajectory data, which leads to poor generalization on out-of-distribution tasks. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a path to learn through environmental interaction, traditional robotics RL often requires hand-crafted, dense reward functions for each task, limiting its scalability and generalizability. Recent work in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) has shown that simple, sparse outcome-based rewards can significantly enhance step-by-step reasoning. This creates an opportunity to apply a similar outcome-driven RL approach to VLAs to overcome the data dependency of SFT and the reward engineering burden of traditional RL.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for robotic manipulation. Despite substantial progress enabled by large-scale pre-training and supervised fine-tuning (SFT), these models face two fundamental challenges: (i) the scarcity and high cost of large-scale robotic trajectories required for SFT scaling, and (ii) limited generalization to tasks under distribution shift. To overcome these limitations, we explore reinforcement learning (RL) as a pathway to scaling VLA training beyond limited datasets. Inspired by LLM breakthroughs where RL with outcome rewards enhances step-by-step reasoning, we ask: Can outcome-driven RL improve long-horizon step-by-step action planning of VLA? In this work, we introduce SimpleVLA-RL, an efficient RL framework tailored for VLA models. Building upon veRL, we introduce VLA-specific trajectory sampling, scalable parallelization, multi-environment rendering, and optimized loss computation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SimpleVLA-RL against SFT-only and other advanced VLA baselines on a range of simulated manipulation benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The SimpleVLA-RL framework will be applied to the OpenVLA-OFT model backbone after an initial SFT stage. Its performance will be compared against a suite of baseline models including UniVLA, RDT-1B, π₀, πfast, Nora, OpenVLA, Octo, DP, and DP3, which are trained with SFT only. The evaluation will be conducted on the LIBERO, RoboTwin1.0, and RoboTwin2.0 benchmarks.

experiments.Description: To investigate whether SimpleVLA-RL can enhance VLA model performance in data-scarce scenarios with extremely limited demonstration data.
experiments.Method: An OpenVLA-OFT model will be fine-tuned on the LIBERO benchmark under two data conditions: 'One-Trajectory SFT' (using only one demonstration per task) and 'Full-Trajectory SFT' (using all 500 demonstrations per task). SimpleVLA-RL will then be applied to the models from both SFT settings. The performance will be compared against the SFT-only models from each condition.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average Success Rate (SR) across LIBERO task suites']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization ability of SimpleVLA-RL on out-of-distribution tasks compared to a standard SFT approach.
experiments.Method: For each of the LIBERO task suites (Spatial, Object, Goal), nine tasks will be selected as 'seen' for training, with one task reserved as 'unseen' for evaluation. A base model will be prepared using the 'One-Trajectory SFT' setting. This base model will then be further trained using two methods: (1) SFT on demonstrations from the 9 seen tasks, and (2) SimpleVLA-RL on scenarios from the 9 seen tasks. The performance of both resulting models will be evaluated on the held-out 'unseen' task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the real-world effectiveness and sim-to-real transfer of policies trained with SimpleVLA-RL.
experiments.Method: An SFT model and an RL model (trained with SimpleVLA-RL) will be developed entirely using simulation data from four RoboTwin2.0 tasks (Stack Bowls, Handover Block, Pick Bottle, Click Bell). These policies will then be deployed on AgileX Piper robotic arms to perform the tasks in the real world. The performance will be compared against the SFT-only model and an RDT baseline model.

risk_factors: RL fails completely when the base model lacks initial task capability (0% success rate).
when initial success rates are too low (< 5%), RL improvements remain negligible, while stronger initial models achieve substantially better final performance after RL training.
The results indicate that a minimal level of task competence is essential for effective RL. Below this threshold, exploration is ineffective and RL fails to produce meaningful gains.

title: LEGATO: Large-scale End-to-end Generalizable Approach to Typeset OMR
name: legato-end-to-end-omr
short_hypothesis: An end-to-end Optical Music Recognition (OMR) model, built upon a pretrained vision encoder and a transformer decoder trained to generate ABC notation, will be capable of recognizing full-page and multi-page typeset music scores and generalizing across various score types. This approach is enabled by training on a newly constructed large-scale dataset of rendered music scores paired with their ABC representations.
related_work: Prior successful end-to-end OMR systems, such as SMT++, have focused exclusively on specific formats like piano, monophonic music, or single-system scores. More generalizable solutions are needed to handle the diversity of inputs, such as complex layouts with multiple systems, staves, and voices, and to support various output formats. To our knowledge, an image-to-ABC OMR model has not yet been trained, and there is no large-scale pretrained model capable of recognizing multi-page typeset scores.
abstract: We propose Legato, a new end-to-end model for optical music recognition (OMR), a task of converting music score images to machine-readable documents. Legato is the first large-scale pretrained OMR model capable of recognizing full-page or multi-page typeset music scores and the first to generate documents in ABC notation, a concise, human-readable format for symbolic music. Bringing together a pretrained vision encoder with an ABC decoder trained on a dataset of more than 214K images, our model is designed to exhibit the strong ability to generalize across various typeset scores.

experiments.Description: To establish the in-domain performance of the Legato models on the output format they were trained to produce.
experiments.Method: The Legato and Legatosmall models are evaluated on the test split of the PDMX-Synth dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['character error rate (CER)', 'symbol error rate (SER)', 'line error rate (LER)']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of Legato on unseen string quartet scores from a different distribution than the training data.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on the OpenScore String Quartets dataset. The evaluation is performed on two subsets: 'Camera' (scanned real scores) and 'Rendered' (clean images generated from MusicXML).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of Legato on unseen Lieder (song) scores.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on the OpenScore Lieder dataset. The evaluation is performed on both 'Camera' (scanned) and 'Rendered' images.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To conduct a realistic evaluation on piano-form camera scores from a source not used in training and produced with older typesetting technology.
experiments.Method: Legato, Legatosmall, SMT++, and GPT-5 are evaluated on a newly manually annotated dataset of full-page piano scores from IMSLP, ensuring no overlap with the training data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to quantify the improvements brought by different design considerations of Legato, such as output format, tokenizer, model architecture, and dataset size.
experiments.Method: The original SMT++ and Legato models are compared against several newly trained variants on the IMSLP Piano Scores dataset. These variants include SMT++ trained on ABC format, an enlarged SMT++large model, and SMT++large trained on the new PDMX-Synth dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Tree Edit Distance with Note Flattening (TEDn)', 'OMR Normalized Edit Distance (OMR-NED)']

risk_factors: Finally, we focus on the recognition of musical notation rather than textual features of a musical score (e.g., lyrics), leaving the textual recognition to future work.
Training autoregressive models on long sequences is computationally expensive, and since such cases are rare, our approach sacrifices them for efficiency.
Finetuning this module [the vision encoder] specifically for scores is a promising direction for future work.
These inputs [multi-page] often result in long sequences that are truncated before reaching the decoder, limiting model performance.

The current OMR-NED implementation is optimized for **kern through syntax correction, while ABC v2.1 lacks parser support; thus, ABC outputs are converted to MusicXML for symbol extraction, which may introduce errors and disadvantage ABC.

title: Evaluating Unsupervised Denoising Requires Unsupervised Metrics
name: unsupervised-denoising-metrics
short_hypothesis: Two novel metrics, the unsupervised mean squared error (uMSE) and the unsupervised peak signal-to-noise ratio (uPSNR), can be computed exclusively from noisy data to evaluate unsupervised denoising methods. These metrics are proposed to be asymptotically consistent estimators of the supervised MSE and PSNR.
related_work: Unsupervised deep-learning denoising methods have shown impressive performance, but their quantitative evaluation is almost completely dominated by benchmarks with simulated noise, as obtaining ground-truth clean images for real-world data is often impossible. This forces practitioners to rely on visual inspection, which prevents systematic hyperparameter optimization. While Stein's unbiased risk estimator (SURE) can estimate MSE without clean data, it has limitations, such as requiring a closed-form expression for the noise likelihood and being computationally expensive. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no unsupervised metrics are currently available to evaluate denoising methods using only noisy data.
abstract: Unsupervised denoising is a crucial challenge in real-world imaging applications. Unsupervised deep-learning methods have demonstrated impressive performance on benchmarks based on synthetic noise. However, no metrics are available to evaluate these methods in an unsupervised fashion. This is highly problematic for the many practical applications where ground-truth clean images are not available. In this work, we propose two novel metrics: the unsupervised mean squared error (uMSE) and the unsupervised peak signal-to-noise ratio (uPSNR), which are computed using only noisy data. We provide a theoretical analysis of these metrics, showing that they are asymptotically consistent estimators of the supervised MSE and PSNR.
