experiments.Description: To conduct a controlled evaluation of the proposed metrics by studying their properties in a scenario where ground-truth clean images are known.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a dataset of natural images (from Martin et al., 2001; Zhang et al., 2017b; Franzen, 1993) corrupted with additive Gaussian noise, and a dataset of simulated electron-microscopy images (from Vincent et al., 2021) corrupted with Poisson noise. For these datasets, the supervised MSE and PSNR will be computed using the ground-truth clean image. These will be compared to the proposed uMSE and uPSNR, which will be computed using noisy references corresponding to the same clean image, and also using references obtained from a single noisy image via spatial subsampling. The metrics will be applied to evaluate several denoising methods: Bilateral filter, DenseNet, DnCNN, UNet, and BlindSpot.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed metrics on a real-world dataset of videos in raw format, contaminated with real noise where no ground-truth is available.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a dataset of videos in raw format from Yue et al. (2020), which contains static objects captured at five different ISO levels. The proposed unsupervised metrics (uMSE and uPSNR), computed using three noisy frames from each video segment, will be compared with MSE and PSNR estimates obtained by averaging ten noisy frames. These metrics will be used to evaluate four different denoisers: a wavelet-based method, temporal averaging, an image-based CNN denoiser (UDVD), and a video-based CNN denoiser (UDVD).

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the application of the proposed metrics for quantitative evaluation of denoisers on real-world transmission electron microscopy (TEM) data in the absence of ground-truth clean images.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a TEM dataset of platinum nanoparticles. An unsupervised Neighbor2Neighbor convolutional neural network will be trained on a portion of the data. Its performance will be compared to a Gaussian-smoothing baseline on two test sets (moderate SNR and low SNR). The evaluation will be performed using the proposed uPSNR metric, computed from a single noisy image via spatial subsampling to handle noise correlation.

risk_factors: These results are limited to denoising under the assumption of independent noise.
How to address the bias introduced by spatial subsampling and achieve an unbiased approximation to the MSE from a single noisy image.
How to account for noise distributions and artifacts which are not pixel-wise independent (see e.g. Prakash et al. (2021)).
How to obtain unsupervised approximations of perceptual metrics such as SSIM Wang et al. (2004).
How to perform unsupervised evaluation for general inverse problems, and related applications such as realistic image synthesis Zwicker et al. (2015).

title: Wide Attention is the Way Forward for Transformers
name: transformer-aspect-ratio
short_hypothesis: An alternative approach to building deep Transformers is to build wider, shallower attention-based models. By systematically changing the model aspect ratio (balancing layers vs. heads per layer) while keeping the total number of attention heads constant, it is hypothesized that wider, single-layer models can equal or outperform their deeper counterparts on NLP tasks when trained from scratch.
related_work: Prior work on Transformer architectures has primarily focused on designing more efficient attention mechanisms or using Network Architecture Search (NAS) to find optimal operator combinations. While widening networks has been shown to be effective for CNNs, and explored to some extent in Transformers, little research has systematically investigated the trade-off between the number of attention layers and the number of attention heads per layer (the 'model aspect ratio'). This coarse-grained design space is not commonly explored in NAS and represents a research gap in understanding optimal Transformer configurations.
abstract: The Transformer is an extremely powerful and prominent deep learning architecture. In this work, we challenge the commonly held belief in deep learning that going deeper is better, and propose an alternative approach that is building wider attention Transformers. We plan to demonstrate that wide single layer Transformer models can typically equal or sometimes outperform deeper ones in a variety of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks when both are trained from scratch. The impact of changing the model aspect ratio on Transformers will be studied systematically. This ratio balances the number of layers and the number of attention heads per layer, while keeping the total number of attention heads and all other hyperparameters constant. We will also evaluate how wide models affect memory footprint, inference speed, and interpretability.

experiments.Description: To systematically investigate the influence of the 'model aspect ratio' on Transformer performance across a variety of NLP tasks and attention mechanisms, when trained from scratch.
experiments.Method: Models will be evaluated on four text classification tasks: IMDb Token Level, IMDb Byte Level, Listops, and Document Matching. For each task, a set of Transformer architectures will be created by varying the number of layers (L) and heads per layer (H) while keeping the total number of heads (L x H) constant. For example, configurations will range from a deep model (e.g., 6 layers, 8 heads) to a wide model (e.g., 1 layer, 48 heads). This comparison will be performed across 10 different attention mechanisms: dot-product, BigBird, Linear Transformer, Linformer, Local attention, Longformer, Performer, Sinkhorn, Sparse Transformer, and Synthesizer. All models will be trained from scratch without pretrained embeddings or parameters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy', 'Model size (number of parameters)', 'Inference latency (on CPU and GPU)', 'Interpretability (via inspection of attention weights)']

experiments.Description: To test whether the findings on model aspect ratio from NLP tasks generalize to the domain of computer vision.
experiments.Method: The Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT-V2-B1) will be used as a baseline on the CIFAR10 dataset. Wider variants of this model will be created by replacing the two attention layers at each of the four stages with a single wide attention layer, thereby reducing depth while increasing width. Two wide variants will be tested: one matching the baseline's total heads, and another with more heads and a larger embedding size to be a closer match in model size.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of models that use a uniform mixture of different attention mechanisms in both wide and deep configurations.
experiments.Method: Models will be constructed using an equal mix of 8 efficient attention mechanisms (BigBird, Linear Transformer, Linformer, Local, Longformer, Performer, Sparse Transformer, and Synthesizer). These mixed-attention models will be tested in both deep (multi-layer) and wide (single-layer) variants on the NLP tasks. The number of heads for each attention type will be scaled to match the total head count of the homogeneous models.

risk_factors: Whilst our study considers the effects of attention width across many attention mechanisms and various tasks, it remains to be seen whether these results hold for much larger models on much larger datasets, for example masked langauage modelling.
As we are not using pretrainining and pretrained embedings, their results are not directly comparable to our own.

The usage of a single wide attention layer in vision Transformers is constrained by the fact that the majority of these vision Transformers still use pooling or convolution layers before the attention.

title: Distribution Shift Detection for Deep Neural Networks
name: Coverage-Based Distribution Shift Detection
short_hypothesis: A distribution shift in a stream of data fed to a deep neural network can be detected efficiently by deriving a tight generalization coverage bound from in-distribution data using selective prediction principles. A systematic violation of this pre-computed bound by the empirical coverage of a new window of data indicates a distribution shift, without needing to perform computationally expensive two-sample tests against the full training dataset.
related_work: Standard methods for distribution shift detection often use a two-step process: dimensionality reduction of the data (e.g., using a model's embeddings) followed by a two-sample statistical test like the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test or Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). These methods, termed black-box shift detection (BBSD), are computationally intensive as they require processing the entire detection-training set for each new window of test data. This makes them impractical for very large datasets. The proposed approach aims to be significantly more efficient by summarizing the training data into a small set of coverage bounds.
abstract: To deploy and operate deep neural models in production, the quality of their predictions, which might be contaminated benignly or manipulated maliciously by input distributional deviations, must be monitored and assessed. Specifically, we study the case of monitoring the healthy operation of a deep neural network (DNN) receiving a stream of data, with the aim of detecting input distributional deviations over which the quality of the network's predictions is potentially damaged. Using selective prediction principles, we propose a distribution deviation detection method for DNNs. The proposed method is derived from a tight coverage generalization bound computed over a sample of instances drawn from the true underlying distribution. Based on this bound, our detector continuously monitors the operation of the network over a test window and fires off an alarm whenever a deviation is detected.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that the proposed coverage bound holds for in-distribution data and is violated as expected when a distribution shift occurs, using a controlled synthetic data environment.
experiments.Method: A simple binary linear classifier will be trained to discriminate between two 2D Gaussians (in-distribution, G_0). Shifted distributions will be created by moving the centers of the Gaussians closer (G_) or farther apart (G+). The detector, using softmax response as its confidence function, will be trained on G_0. Test windows will be drawn from G_0, G_, and G+ to check if the lower and a symmetric upper coverage bound are violated as hypothesized.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The relationship between the empirical coverages, the computed bounds, and the desired coverages will be plotted to visually inspect for bound violations. The p-value from the detection test will also be considered.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed coverage-based detection method against baseline methods on the CIFAR-10 dataset under various simulated distribution shifts.
experiments.Method: Pretrained ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 models will be used. The in-distribution (ID) data is CIFAR-10. Distribution shifts will be simulated using Rotations, Gaussian noise, the CIFAR-100 dataset, the SVHN dataset, and adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD, CW). The proposed method will be tested with two confidence-rate functions: Softmax Response (Ours-SR) and Soft-Entropy (Ours-Ent). These will be compared against four baselines: KS-BBSD-S, KS-BBSD-E, MMD-BBSD-S, and MMD-BBSD-E.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Area Under an Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC), Area under the Precision-Recall curve for in-distribution data (AUPR-Tr), and Area under the Precision-Recall curve for out-of-distribution data (AUPR-Te). Aggregated versions of these metrics over all window sizes will also be used (Agg-AUROC, Agg-AUPR-Tr, Agg-AUPR-Te).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed detection method on the large-scale ImageNet dataset against the best-performing baseline, considering various types of distribution shifts and different levels of contamination in the test window.
experiments.Method: A pretrained EfficientNet-B0 model will be used. The in-distribution (ID) data is ImageNet. Distribution shifts will be simulated using Rotations, ImageNet-O, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-C datasets, and adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD, CW). The experiment will also vary the proportion of shifted instances (p = 1, 2/3, 1/3) in the test window. The proposed method (Ours-SR and Ours-Ent) will be compared against the best baseline from the previous experiment, KS-BBSD-S.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Area Under an Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC), Area under the Precision-Recall curve for in-distribution data (AUPR-Tr), and Area under the Precision-Recall curve for out-of-distribution data (AUPR-Te). Aggregated versions of these metrics will also be reported.

risk_factors: Although we only considered classification, our method can be extended to regression using an appropriate confidence-rate function such as the MC-dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016).
Extensions to other tasks, such as object detection and segmentation, would be very interesting.
It would also be interesting to examine other types of shift benchmarks such as (Koh et al., 2021).
In our method, the information from the multiple coverage bounds was aggregated by averaging, but it is plausible that other statistics or weighted averages could provide more effective detections.
Finally, an interesting open question is whether one can benefit from using outlier/adversarial detection techniques combined with population-based detection techniques (as discussed here).

title: Wasserstein Hypergraph Neural Network
name: wasserstein-hypergraph-neural-network
short_hypothesis: Treating node and hyperedge neighborhoods as distributions and aggregating them using Sliced Wasserstein Pooling will capture geometric properties (like shape and spread) that are lost by conventional sum-based aggregators, leading to more effective hypergraph representations.
related_work: Most existing hypergraph neural networks (e.g., HGNN, HCHA, AllDeepSets, Set Transformers) fall under a two-stage, set-based framework but rely on simple sum-based or mean-based pooling operations for aggregation. While theoretically powerful, these aggregators only capture first-order statistics and can fail to preserve the geometric structure of the node and hyperedge feature distributions, which is argued to be highly relevant for hypergraph learning.
abstract: While graph representation learning has become mainstream, representing higher-order relationships through hypergraphs is rapidly gaining momentum. Numerous hypergraph neural networks have emerged, most falling under a two-stage, set-based framework where messages are passed between nodes and edges. However, most of these models simplify the aggregation step to basic pooling operations like mean or sum, which only capture first-order statistics. This paper introduces the Wasserstein Hypergraph Neural Network (WHNN), a model that treats node and hyperedge neighborhoods as distributions and aggregates information using Sliced Wasserstein Pooling. Unlike conventional aggregators, this approach is designed to preserve geometric properties like the shape and spread of distributions, enabling learned embeddings to reflect how easily one hyperedge distribution can be transformed into another, following principles of optimal transport.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Wasserstein Hypergraph Neural Network (WHNN) on the node classification task against a range of strong baseline methods.
experiments.Method: The model will be tested on ten real-world datasets: Cora, Citeseer, Cora-CA, DBLP-CA, ModelNet40, NTU2012, 20News, Senate, Congress, and House. Two variants of WHNN will be evaluated: one with an edge-independent MLP encoder (WHNN_MLP) and one with an edge-dependent SAB encoder (WHNN_(I)SAB). The baselines for comparison will include HCHA, HNHN, HyperGCN, HyperGNN, AllDeepSets, AllSetTransformers, UniGCNII, and ED-HNN.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to isolate and understand the contribution of the Wasserstein aggregator compared to other common aggregation methods used in hypergraph networks.
experiments.Method: The underlying model architecture (with both MLP and SAB encoders) will be kept fixed while varying the aggregator. The proposed Sliced Wasserstein Pooling aggregator (with both fixed and learnable reference distributions, denoted FPSWE and LPSWE) will be compared against Deep Sets and PMA aggregators. This will be tested on the Citeseer, NTU2012, Cora_CA, and ModelNet40 datasets.

experiments.Description: To investigate how the choice of node encoder (edge-dependent vs. edge-independent) affects the overall performance of the proposed model.
experiments.Method: The performance of the WHNN model equipped with an edge-independent MLP encoder will be compared against the same model equipped with an edge-dependent self-attention block (SAB) encoder across multiple datasets.

experiments.Description: To validate the hypothesis that the specific choice of reference distribution and the number of sampled points have minimal impact on performance, as the reference distribution acts as a shared anchor.
experiments.Method: A series of experiments will be conducted on the Citeseer, Cora_CA, and NTU datasets. The shape of the reference distribution will be varied (uniform, Gaussian, Poisson, learnable) and the number of sampled reference points will be modified ({2, 5, 10, 25, 50}).

risk_factors: Consistent with findings in set representation learning, employing a more sophisticated aggregator, such as the one introduced in this work, inevitably increases computational overhead.

Due to this continuous view of the neighbourhood (as a distribution of probability) together with the interpolation step, the current model may lose information about the exact cardinality of the neighbourhoods.

This approach assumes that any additional nodes drawn from this distribution should belong to the same neighbourhood as the observed ones. This aligns with the intuition that elements within a group should share common characteristics. While the datasets we used support this assumption, there may be real-world scenarios where it does not hold.

title: Schrodinger's Memory: Large Language Models
name: schrodingers-memory
short_hypothesis: The memory mechanism in Large Language Models (LLMs) can be explained by the Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT), where the Transformer architecture functions as a dynamic UAT model that adaptively fits outputs based on inputs. This memory is termed 'Schrödinger's memory' because it only becomes observable when queried. A model's memory ability, assessed by its capacity to recall information, can serve as an objective measure of its overall capabilities.
related_work: Previous research on LLM memory has primarily focused on two approaches: expanding the context length to provide more information across long sequences, and integrating external memory storage systems for retrieval and updates. However, these studies have not fully explained the intrinsic memory mechanisms within the models themselves, lacking a fundamental theoretical framework and resulting in a vague definition of memory.
abstract: With the development of Large Language Models (LLMs), their language capabilities are becoming increasingly comparable to those of humans. But do LLMs have memory? Based on current practice, LLMs do appear to exhibit memory. So, what is the underlying mechanism of this memory? Previous research lacked a deep exploration of LLMs' memory capabilities and the underlying theory. In this paper, we use the Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT) to explain the memory mechanism in LLMs. We also conduct experiments to verify the memory capabilities of various LLMs, proposing a new method to assess their abilities based on the memory ability. We argue that LLM memory operates like Schrödinger's memory, meaning that it only becomes observable when a specific memory is queried. We can only determine if the model retains a memory based on its output in response to the query; otherwise, it remains indeterminate.

experiments.Description: To verify the memory capabilities of various LLMs by fine-tuning them on a poem memorization task and testing their ability to recite poems from minimal input cues.
experiments.Method: Models from the Qwen series (Qwen1.5-0.5B-Chat, Qwen2-0.5B-Instruct, Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct) and bloom series (bloom-389m-zh, bloom-1b4-zh, bloom-560m, bloom-1b7) are to be fine-tuned on the CN Poems and ENG Poems datasets. The task involves generating the full content of a poem given its metadata (dynasty, author, and title for Chinese poems; author and title for English poems). The models will be trained for 100 epochs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, defined as the percentage of test examples where the predicted poem content exactly matches the ground truth.']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of output text length on the memory capabilities of LLMs, under the hypothesis that longer text is more difficult to remember.
experiments.Method: Models (Qwen1.5-0.5B-Chat, Qwen2-0.5B-Instruct, Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct, bloom-389m-zh, bloom-1b4-zh) are to be fine-tuned for 100 epochs on a version of the CN Poems dataset where the combined length of the input and output text is set to be between 256 and 512 characters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy, defined as the percentage of test examples where the predicted poem content exactly matches the ground truth.']

risk_factors: Due to the complexity of the brain, many operational mechanisms remain unclear, and no reasonable conclusions currently exist regarding its specific workings.

title: EasyTune: Efficient Step-Aware Fine-Tuning for Diffusion-Based Motion Generation
name: easytune-step-aware-finetuning
short_hypothesis: Fine-tuning a diffusion model at each individual denoising step, rather than over the entire trajectory, will decouple the recursive gradient dependence, enabling more memory-efficient, dense, and fine-grained optimization. Furthermore, a self-refinement preference learning mechanism can dynamically construct preference pairs from a retrieval model's failures to train a high-quality reward model without human annotation.
related_work: Existing differentiable reward methods for aligning diffusion models (e.g., DRaFT, DRTune) suffer from inefficiency, high memory usage, and coarse-grained optimization. This is attributed to backpropagating gradients through the entire multi-step denoising trajectory, which creates a recursive dependence that causes vanishing gradients for early steps and requires storing a large computation graph. Additionally, methods for training reward models are hampered by the scarcity of high-quality, large-scale preference data for motion generation.
abstract: Aligning motion generative models with downstream objectives is challenging. While using differentiable rewards to align diffusion models is promising, existing methods suffer from inefficient, coarse-grained optimization and high memory consumption, which is caused by the recursive dependence between steps in the denoising trajectory. This work proposes EasyTune, a method to fine-tune diffusion models at each denoising step rather than over the entire trajectory, decoupling the recursive dependence for more efficient optimization. To address the scarcity of preference data for reward model training, a Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) mechanism is also introduced to dynamically identify preference pairs for learning.

experiments.Description: To assess the fine-tuning effectiveness and efficiency of EasyTune compared to state-of-the-art differentiable reward-based methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the HumanML3D dataset. The MLD model will be fine-tuned using EasyTune and benchmarked against baselines including DRaFT, AlignProp, and DRTune.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['R-Precision@k', 'Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Multi-Modal Distance (MM Dist)', 'Diversity', 'peak memory usage']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of EasyTune when applied to base models for the end-to-end text-to-motion generation task, comparing against state-of-the-art generation models.
experiments.Method: The MLD and MLD++ models will be fine-tuned with EasyTune. Performance will be evaluated on the HumanML3D and KIT-ML datasets. Baselines will include a wide range of text-to-motion models such as TM2T, T2M, MDM, ReMoDiffuse, ParCo, and SoPo.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization capability of the EasyTune framework across various pre-trained text-to-motion diffusion models.
experiments.Method: EasyTune will be applied to a set of different pre-trained diffusion-based architectures, including MLD, MLD++, MLCM1S, and MDM50S, to assess its performance improvement on each.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Self-refinement Preference Learning (SPL) mechanism for training a reward model for the text-motion retrieval task.
experiments.Method: The SPL mechanism will be used to fine-tune a pre-trained retrieval model (ReAlign). Its performance will be evaluated on the HumanML3D and KIT-ML datasets and compared against the original ReAlign model and other state-of-the-art retrieval methods like LaMP and TMR.

risk_factors: In this work, we focus on improving semantic alignment in text-to-motion generation.
Due to limited preference data, SPL relies on retrieval-based mining, which can introduce noisy or ambiguous pairs and provides limited physical grounding in reward design.
Building a unified reward model that explicitly captures both physical plausibility and semantic alignment remains an important direction for future work.

title: On the Surprising Efficacy of Online Self-Improvement for Embodied Multimodal Foundation Models
name: online-self-improvement-for-embodied-mfas
short_hypothesis: A two-stage fine-tuning approach for Multimodal Foundation Agents (MFAs) in robotics, consisting of an initial Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) stage followed by an Online Self-Improvement (RL) stage, will be more sample-efficient and produce more performant policies than SFT alone. The second stage can leverage the model's own predictions of 'steps-to-go' to derive an effective reward function, eliminating the need for manual reward engineering.
related_work: The application of foundation models to low-level robot control has been largely limited to behavioral cloning (e.g., RT-2). While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great success in fine-tuning LLMs, its application to robotics is hindered by challenges like sample efficiency and manual reward engineering. This work aims to bridge this gap by designing a two-stage framework inspired by LLM post-training processes, specifically for robotic MFAs, to enable sample-efficient online improvement without ground-truth rewards.
abstract: Foundation models trained on web-scale data have revolutionized robotics, but their application to low-level control remains largely limited to behavioral cloning. Drawing inspiration from the sample efficiency and success of reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning in large language models (LLMs), we propose a two-stage approach suited to robotics. The first stage, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), fine-tunes pre-trained foundation models using goal-conditioned behavioral cloning and “steps-to-go” prediction objectives. In the second stage, this foundation enables the extraction of a well-shaped reward function and a success detector, eliminating the need for manual reward engineering and real-world instrumentation, and allowing robots to practice autonomously with minimal human supervision.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if the proposed self-improvement procedure improves performance beyond the supervised learning stage and is more sample-efficient than supervised learning alone in a simulated environment.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the simulated LanguageTable domain. The PaLI MFA will be trained on subsets (10%, 20%, 80%) of the human-generated trajectory dataset. Stage 1 involves supervised fine-tuning with behavioral cloning and 'steps-to-go' prediction objectives. Stage 2 involves online self-improvement (RL) on the 'Block2Block' tasks. The performance of the two-stage model will be compared against the Stage 1 policy baseline (equivalent to RT-2).

experiments.Description: To test if the self-improvement procedure is reliable and reproducible enough to be employed for real-world robotics.
experiments.Method: The two-stage framework will be applied to the real-world LanguageTable domain, using 20% and 80% of the real-world dataset for Stage 1. Stage 2 fine-tuning will be performed on the 'Block2Block' subset of tasks, with one human operator monitoring 3-4 robot stations simultaneously.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed fine-tuning framework on a second, more complex robot embodiment, the bimanual Aloha manipulation platform.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a simulated Aloha platform for a bimanual insertion task. Imitation datasets of 5K, 10K, and 15K trajectories will be used. The two-stage process will be applied to the 5K and 10K datasets and compared against supervised learning baselines (Stage 1 only) trained on 10K and 15K episodes.

experiments.Description: To ablate the contribution of the web-scale pre-training of the foundation model to the effectiveness of Stage 2 self-improvement.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be run on the simulated LanguageTable domain. The Stage 2 policy will be initialized from a PaLI Stage 1 checkpoint, but the reward model will be derived from different model variations: 1) a fully pretrained PaLI model, 2) a 'Frankenstein' model (pretrained ViT and language Transformer without joint co-training), and 3) a 'Scratch' model (randomly initialized architecture).

experiments.Description: To investigate the framework's ability to enable domain transfer, specifically Real2Sim transfer.
experiments.Method: Stage 1 models will be trained using 80% of the real-world LanguageTable dataset. Then, Stage 2 self-improvement will be performed in the simulated LanguageTable environment. This will also be compared to using a 'Frankenstein' reward model to highlight the role of pre-training in domain transfer.

experiments.Description: To test the generalization ability of the self-improvement approach to learn novel skills not present in the initial imitation dataset.
experiments.Method: Starting from a real-world LanguageTable policy already fine-tuned for 'Block2Block' tasks, further Stage 2 fine-tuning will be performed on the 'BananaTable' task. This task replaces the blocks with a prosthetic banana and requires pushing it, a visually and behaviorally distinct skill not seen by the policy or reward model before.

risk_factors: Our approach uses on-policy REINFORCE for simplicity which does not reuse any collected data in Stage 2. Off-policy methods have the potential to even more substantially improve Stage 2 sample-efficiency.

Training large models requires significant compute budgets. Understanding whether our framework is amenable to parameter-efficient tuning methods (e.g. LoRA (Hu et al., 2021)) is critical towards enabling broad access to Stage 2 fine-tuning.

What are the failure cases of our reward formulation? Anecdotally, we have seen that if we continue training Stage 2 for past the peak performance, the success rate of the policies can begin to degrade. Is this due to gaps between our reward formulation and intended task outcome?

title: Exploring General Intelligence of Program Analysis for Multiple Tasks
name: multi-graph-assembly-embedding
short_hypothesis: A program embedding methodology based on assembly code, which represents a program using multiple graph structures (Control Flow Graph, Call Graph, and Data Flow Graph), can create a general representation suitable for diverse program analysis tasks, including both semantics-based and compilation-related problems.
related_work: Previous machine learning approaches for program analysis often treat code as text sequences or use Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs), which ignore rich structural information and long-distance dependencies. Other methods that use Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) typically rely on a single graph structure, such as a control flow graph. Furthermore, representations built from source code or ASTs lack compile-time and architecture-specific information, making them unsuitable for tasks like binary similarity detection across different compilation configurations. This work aims to address this gap by creating a program representation from assembly code that fuses information from multiple graph structures (CFG, CG, DFG) to capture richer structural and semantic details, including compilation-specific characteristics.
abstract: As the program scale ever grows, program analysis tasks become increasingly diverse and complex. Effective learning-based program analysis tasks need to consider many factors including not only program structures and semantics but also running platforms and compilation configurations. Existing works are oftentimes on a basis of source code or abstract syntax tree, but unfortunately lack sufficient program structure and semantic information to unveil program insights. To this end, we propose a new program analysis model that takes advantage of graph neural networks at the level of assembly code. The model aims to solve both traditional program semantics-based problems and compilation-related problems by fusing the semantics of the program's instructions and the existence of multiple graph structures within programs.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to capture the key functional information of a program, it will be tested on a program classification task.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the POJ-104 dataset, which contains programs from 104 different classes. Programs will be compiled using GCC and LLVM with -O2 and -O3 optimization levels. The proposed model (MTM) will generate an embedding for each program, which is then fed to a softmax classifier. The performance will be compared against the Ins2vec baseline. Ablation studies are also planned to evaluate the contribution of individual graph components (CFG, CG, DFG).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to handle compilation-related problems, it will be tested on binary similarity detection, which is composed of two subtasks: cross-configuration binary code detection and compilation option classification.
experiments.Method: Using binaries generated from the POJ-104 dataset, the first subtask will train the model in a siamese framework to predict if two binaries, compiled with different configurations, originate from the same source code. Performance will be compared to the Order Matters Model (OM). The second subtask will be a multi-class classification task to predict the compilation option (GCC-O2, GCC-O3, LLVM-O2, LLVM-O3) of a given binary.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Similarity distance (for cross-configuration detection)', 'Classification accuracy (for compilation classification)']

risk_factors: Furthermore, these graphs are relative large for the efficient graph-based deep learning models (e.g., graph neural networks) to run. As tested, the number of nodes and edges of the DFG can be as high as 10847 nodes and 26910 edges. These large-scale graphs incur tediously long training time using graph neural networks.
However, procedure-level analysis tasks such as function name prediction can also be solved by the proposed model, which we will leave as future work.

title: Clearer Frames, Anytime: Resolving Velocity Ambiguity in Video Frame Interpolation
name: distance-indexing-vfi
short_hypothesis: Instead of implicitly learning a complicated time-to-location mapping via a scalar timestep, explicitly providing a Video Frame Interpolation (VFI) network with a 'distance index'—a map indicating how far an object has traveled—will resolve speed ambiguity and lead to sharper interpolated frames. Furthermore, an iterative reference-based estimation strategy, which breaks long-range predictions into shorter, easier steps, will resolve the remaining directional ambiguity.
related_work: Existing learning-based Video Frame Interpolation (VFI) methods, for both fixed-time (t=0.5) and arbitrary-time (t ∈ [0, 1]) paradigms, use a scalar timestep 't' to specify the target frame. This approach suffers from 'velocity ambiguity,' where a single input set (start frame, end frame, t) can correspond to multiple valid ground-truth frames depending on the object's unknown acceleration and trajectory. This one-to-many mapping during training causes models to learn an average of the possibilities, resulting in blurry and imprecise interpolated frames. This paper aims to explicitly resolve this ambiguity rather than relying on the model to learn it implicitly.
abstract: Existing video frame interpolation (VFI) methods blindly predict where each object is at a specific timestep t ('time indexing'), which struggles to predict precise object movements, often resulting in blurry frames as the method averages out possibilities. Instead of forcing the network to learn this complicated time-to-location mapping implicitly, we provide the network with an explicit hint on how far the object has traveled between start and end frames, a novel approach termed 'distance indexing'. This method offers a clearer learning goal for models, reducing the uncertainty tied to object speeds. To solve the remaining directional ambiguity in long-range motion, especially halfway between frames, we propose an iterative reference-based estimation strategy that breaks down a long-range prediction into several short-range steps.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed 'distance indexing' and 'iterative reference-based estimation' strategies as plug-and-play modules on existing arbitrary time VFI models.
experiments.Method: The strategies will be integrated into four state-of-the-art VFI models: RIFE, IFRNet, AMT, and EMA-VFI. All models will be trained on the Vimeo90K septuplet dataset. The experiment will compare the performance of the baseline model using time indexing ([T]) against enhanced versions incorporating distance indexing ([D]), iterative reference-based estimation ([T, R]), and a combination of both ([D, R]).

experiments.Description: To validate the perceptual quality improvement of the proposed strategies through a human evaluation study.
experiments.Method: A user study with 30 anonymous participants will be conducted. Participants will be tasked with ranking the interpolation quality of frames produced by the four model variants: the baseline ([T]), distance indexing ([D]), iterative reference-based estimation ([T,R]), and the combined approach ([D,R]).

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of the proposed strategies on different benchmarks and for higher interpolation factors.
experiments.Method: The RIFE model, enhanced with the proposed strategies, will be evaluated on the Adobe240 and X4K1000FPS datasets for ×8 and ×16 interpolation tasks. Inference will be performed using uniform distance maps.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the novel capability of using distance indexing for per-object temporal manipulation, or 'manipulated interpolation of anything'.
experiments.Method: Object masks will be produced for a starting frame using the Segment Anything Model (SAM). Users can then customize the distance curve for each object mask to control its interpolation pattern (e.g., making one person move backward in time). The VFI model will then generate an interpolated sequence based on these specified, spatially-varying distance maps.

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed distance indexing approach against traditional time indexing in a fixed-time (t=0.5) training setting.
experiments.Method: The RIFE model will be trained on the Vimeo90K triplet dataset using both traditional time indexing and the proposed distance indexing approach.

risk_factors: Firstly, the current system only allows for manipulations along a fixed trajectory. It does not possess the capability to model the distribution of possible trajectories.

In our current approach, we use a uniform distance map which assumes every object moves at constant speed along their trajectories. In practice, different objects may have different acceleration and therefore this constant speed assumption may lead to sub-optimal frame interpolation.

The overall effectiveness and accuracy of the model are intrinsically tied to the precision of optical flow estimation and the quality of segmentation masks (optional). Any inaccuracies or shortcomings in these preliminary steps can cascade and potentially impact the final interpolation results.

title: Factored Representation for Neuro-Symbolic AI
name: factored-representation-for-neuro-symbolic-ai
short_hypothesis: Forcing a pre-trained LLM to maintain a structured internal state using a formal representation can lead to comparable reasoning performance, but with significantly improved interpretability, making the model's thought process more transparent and verifiable.
related_work: Large language models (LLMs) are powerful but their internal decision-making is opaque and unverifiable, posing a challenge to trustworthiness. The field of Neuro-Symbolic AI aims to bridge data-driven neural networks with structured symbolic systems to create more robust and interpretable models. A key focus is knowledge representation. While prior work has integrated symbolic methods by altering the internal architecture of neural networks, this research explores applying structured representations to pre-trained LLMs through prompting and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, without requiring architectural changes.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of tasks, yet their internal decision-making processes remain largely opaque, posing a significant challenge to their trustworthiness in complex reasoning scenarios. We explore the hypothesis that compelling a pre-trained LLM to maintain a structured internal state using a formal, symbolic representation can enhance interpretability without degrading its reasoning performance. To investigate this, we employ two primary techniques: prompt fine-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) using LoRA. Thus, we prompt a variety of LLMs to articulate their reasoning steps using various structured formalisms, including basic semantic triples, lists of attribute-value pairs, and first-order logic. We also fine-tune a pre-trained LLM on a structured representation that the LLM is subsequently prompted to use as an internal representation during reasoning to solve a task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of prompting LLMs to use various formal, structured representations for their reasoning steps on their core reasoning capabilities.
experiments.Method: Pre-trained LLMs (Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and gpt-oss-20b) are prompted using zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT) techniques. The prompts instruct the models to utilize different formalisms: First-Order Logic (FOL), Propositional Logic (Prop), semantic triples (SPO), and alists. The models' performance is then evaluated on reasoning benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the GSM8K and MMLU benchmarks.', 'Adherence score, measuring the percentage of model outputs that contain the prompted representation.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate if fine-tuning an LLM on a specific structured representation (alists) improves its ability to use that structure and affects its reasoning performance.
experiments.Method: The Qwen3-4B model is fine-tuned using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with LoRA on a custom-generated dataset (ALIST8.5K) of natural language and alist pairs. The resulting fine-tuned models (Qwen3-4B-ALIST-16 and Qwen3-4B-ALIST-32) are then evaluated on reasoning benchmarks, both with and without being explicitly prompted to use the alist format.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the GSM8K and MMLU benchmarks.', "Adherence to the alist representation in the model's output."]

risk_factors: Our results and evaluation are restricted by our use of only two reasoning benchmarks.
A key challenge we came up against in our research was the lack of verification of models' outputs.
Another limitation of our work is the dataset we've used for fine-tuning. The quality of our dataset is entirely dependent on the capabilities of our chosen model – in this case OpenAI's o4-mini.
In this work, we only explore prompt fine-tuning and LoRA-based finetuning.

title: ECAttention: Adaptive and Principled Feature Expansion-Compression with Linear Efficiency
name: ECAttention
short_hypothesis: A novel attention mechanism, ECAttention, can be designed based on the geometric insight of the MCR² objective to achieve effective and principled feature expansion and compression. By operating on the column and null spaces of the feature matrix, and using randomization with Cholesky decomposition, this method can achieve nearly linear complexity. Furthermore, by introducing trainable weights for expansion and compression modules, the model can self-adaptively balance these two forces across layers.
related_work: The maximal coding rate reduction (MCR²) objective provides a principled framework for learning low-dimensional subspace representations. Prior work like ReduNet, which directly derives operators from the MCR² gradient, suffers from scalability issues. More recent methods like CRATE and ToST improve scalability but introduce limitations. CRATE's compression module (MSSA) is theoretically flawed as it increases the compression term it aims to minimize. ToST achieves linear complexity by using only the diagonal elements of the feature correlation matrix, but this neglects correlations between dimensions, failing to capture fine-grained data structures. Both approaches also lack an explicit expansion module and a self-adaptive mechanism to balance expansion and compression across layers.
abstract: The maximal coding rate reduction (MCR²) objective has been proposed to learn low-dimensional subspace representations by minimizing the compression term for intra-group compactness and maximizing the expansion term for inter-group separation. Several studies have leveraged MCR² to design principled, interpretable deep models by following or approximating its gradient to derive layer structures. However, these approaches remain limited in achieving fully principled and effective compression and lack self-adaptive control over the strength of expansion and compression across layers. In this work, we introduce ECAttention, a novel attention mechanism that incorporates principled expansion and compression modules inspired by the geometric insight of MCR². Geometrically, gradient-based updates of MCR² move features along directions shaped by the underlying data structure. Our method proposes to efficiently capture this structure using randomization combined with Cholesky decomposition to guide feature updates with nearly linear complexity. By introducing two trainable weights per layer, ECAttention is designed to self-adaptively regulate the strengths of compression and expansion.

experiments.Description: To validate whether the proposed expansion and compression modules achieve their intended effective operation of expanding and compressing feature representations.
experiments.Method: Synthetic toy data is generated from K mutually orthogonal subspaces. The proposed expansion and compression modules are applied separately to this data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Layer-wise coding rate for expansion R(Z)', 'Layer-wise coding rate for compression Rc(Z|U[K])', 'Cumulative distribution of eigenvalues (CDE) of the feature covariance matrix ZZT']

experiments.Description: To determine if compressing features by subtracting the null-space projection is a more stable and effective optimization strategy compared to directly projecting onto the column space.
experiments.Method: Two compression strategies are compared: 1) subtracting the null-space projection, and 2) directly projecting onto the column space. The experiment is conducted on a toy dataset.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the task performance and interpretability of the ECA transformer on real-world visual data against existing methods.
experiments.Method: ECA models of varying scales (ECA-S, ECA-M, ECA-B+, etc.) are pre-trained on the ImageNet-1K dataset and subsequently fine-tuned on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Oxford Flowers-102, and Oxford-IIT-Pets datasets. The performance is to be compared against ToST, CRATE, and DeiT baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Top-1 accuracy', 'Visualization of head-wise membership distribution (attention maps)']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the ECA transformer exhibits self-adaptive behavior in balancing expansion and compression across layers.
experiments.Method: The ECA-S model is trained on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiment involves varying the rank hyperparameter 'r' of the random matrix used for column space approximation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Analysis of the learned expansion weights (alpha) across layers', 'Analysis of the learned compression weights (beta) across layers']

risk_factors: Although the framework shows some adaptability to random matrix size, this study does not quantify the potential information loss under varying matrix sizes.
Besides, due to limitations in computational resources and time, it remains to be investigated whether our framework can achieve strong performance on larger-scale tasks.

title: Unified Neural Network Scaling Laws and Scale-time Equivalence
name: scale-time-equivalence
short_hypothesis: Scaling the size of a neural network is functionally equivalent to increasing its training time by a proportional factor. This 'scale-time equivalence' can be combined with a linear model analysis of double descent to create a unified scaling law that explains phenomena like parameter-wise double descent as an effect of early noise acquisition by models that train effectively faster.
related_work: Existing neural network scaling laws characterize how test error varies with network size and data volume, often as a power law, but they are applicable only in limited regimes and do not incorporate training time or phenomena like double descent. Conversely, theories of double descent explain non-monotonic error curves with respect to model size or data volume, but often fail to explain its behavior with respect to training time or why it is not always observed empirically. This work seeks to unify these two lines of research into a single framework that accounts for model size, training time, and data volume.
abstract: Existing neural network scaling laws, which characterize how test error varies with network size and data volume, are often applicable only in limited regimes and do not incorporate phenomena such as double descent. This work presents a novel theoretical characterization of how model size, training time, and data volume interact. We propose a theoretical and empirical equivalence between scaling the size of a neural network and increasing its training time proportionally ('scale-time equivalence'). This concept challenges the practice of training large models for short durations and suggests smaller models trained longer could be as effective. We combine scale-time equivalence with a linear model analysis of double descent to obtain a unified theoretical scaling law.

experiments.Description: To empirically validate the proposed 'scale-time equivalence' by examining the trade-off between model scale and the training time required for a neural network to generalize.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN datasets. Two architectures will be used: a 7-layer CNN and a 6-layer MLP, trained with SGD. Model scale will be varied by adjusting network width. The minimum amount of training time (epochs) required to achieve non-zero generalization will be measured for various network widths and dataset subsample sizes.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Trade-off curves between model scale (defined as the effective number of parameters) and training time (epochs).

experiments.Description: To test whether scale-time equivalence can be used to extrapolate and predict the performance of neural networks across different scales and training durations.
experiments.Method: Two sets of predictions will be made on benchmark datasets (e.g., MNIST) with CNN and MLP architectures. First, the performance of larger models will be predicted by training smaller models for extended training times (e.g., 100 epochs). Second, the performance of smaller models over many epochs will be predicted by training larger models for a short duration (e.g., 1 epoch). The quantity 'pt' (effective parameters * time) will be used as the basis for extrapolation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Predicted vs. true test and train Mean Squared Error (MSE) curves, plotted against model scale or number of epochs.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis, derived from scale-time equivalence, that larger models require less data to generalize, which contrasts with predictions from conventional double descent theory.
experiments.Method: CNN models of varying scales will be trained on the MNIST dataset with varying amounts of training data. The amount of data required for the model to start generalizing (i.e., for test error to decrease) will be observed for each model scale.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Test and train Mean Squared Error (MSE) as a function of data volume for different model scales.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that label noise causes a persistent or growing error with increasing model scale and training time, as opposed to a localized error increase near the interpolation threshold predicted by conventional double descent theories.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be run on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN using CNN and MLP architectures. Models will be trained with and without label noise (20%) added to the training set. Performance will be evaluated as a function of training epochs, model scale, and training data size.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Test Mean Squared Error (MSE) curves under noisy vs. noise-free conditions.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that, under certain noise conditions, the test error curve can be U-shaped with respect to model scale, where performance worsens for very large models, contrary to conventional double descent theory.
experiments.Method: Models (CNNs and MLPs) will be trained on CIFAR-10 and SVHN. The test error will be plotted as a function of model scale, extending into the highly overparameterized regime.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Test Mean Squared Error (MSE) as a function of model scale.

risk_factors: Our experiments are all conducted on standard vision benchmarks; we believe testing our theory on the language domain is a critical future direction.
Another limitation is that we do not yet have a fundamental understanding of what sets the appropriate model scale of a neural network.
Experimentally, we found that the cube root of the number of model parameters is appropriate, but without a strong theoretical basis; this deserves further study.

title: Learning Constraints from Offline Dataset via Inverse Dual Values Estimation
name: IDVE for Offline ICRL
short_hypothesis: By reformulating offline Inverse Constraint Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) as a regularized policy learning problem, it is possible to learn constraints from a static offline dataset. This can be achieved by deriving dual value functions for both rewards and costs and formulating a bi-level optimization problem to estimate their values. This approach, named Inverse Dual Values Estimation (IDVE), is designed to ensure safety and conservatism by regularizing the policy's deviation from the expert policy and incentivizing it to operate within the distribution of the offline data.
related_work: Classical Inverse Constraint Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) algorithms infer constraints from expert demonstrations but typically require an online learning paradigm with boundless exploration. This is often dangerous and expensive in realistic, safety-critical applications. Existing offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods focus on learning policies or rewards from static datasets but have not been applied to the problem of offline constraint inference. This work presents itself as the first attempt to create a solution for offline ICRL, addressing the gap where no prior method exists.
abstract: To develop safe control strategies, Inverse Constrained Reinforcement learning (ICRL) infers constraints from expert demonstrations. Classical ICRL algorithms typically adopt an online learning diagram that permits boundless exploration, which is dangerous and expensive for safe-critical control tasks. To address this challenge, this work presents a novel Inverse Dual Values Estimation (IDVE) framework for offline ICRL. IDVE dynamically combines the conservative estimation inherent in offline RL and the data-driven inference in inverse RL to learn constraints from limited data. Specifically, IDVE derives dual value functions for both rewards and costs, estimating their values in a bi-level optimization problem based on the offline dataset. To derive a practical algorithm, the paper introduces methods for handling unknown transitions, scaling to continuous environments, and controlling the degree of sparsity regularization.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the algorithm's ability to recover constraints and visualize the learned value functions in discrete environments.
experiments.Method: The method is evaluated on 7x7 discrete grid-world maps, each with four unique constraint settings. The offline dataset for each environment consists of 50 trajectories, including expert demonstrations and sub-optimal data from random walks. The proposed method (IDVE) is compared against several baselines: IDVE w/o S (without sparsity control), IDVE w/o A (without the violating action buffer), Offline IL (Inverse Soft Q-Learning), and Offline RL (f-DVL).

experiments.Description: To study how well the constraints inferred from training environments can be generalized to new, unseen environments with different starting and target states.
experiments.Method: Constraints are learned using data from the initial grid-world environments. The resulting policies are then evaluated on new grid-world settings with different start and target states. The performance of IDVE is compared against IDVE w/oS and Offline RL. The Offline IL baseline is omitted for this experiment.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's performance on continuous control tasks with predefined physical constraints.
experiments.Method: The experiments are conducted on four modified MuJoCo environments: Limited Speed Ant, Limit Arm HalfCheetah, Blocked Walker, and Blocked Half-Cheetah. The offline dataset is created from an early-stopped SAC replay buffer combined with expert trajectories. The proposed method (IDVE) is compared with its ablations (IDVE w/oA, IDVE w/oS) and other baselines (Offline RL, Offline IL).

risk_factors: A promising avenue for future research involves extending our method to accommodate diverse ICRL configurations, such as soft constraints in stochastic environments.

title: Contextual Fusion For Adversarial Robustness
name: contextual-fusion-adversarial-robustness
short_hypothesis: Fusing features from two parallel data streams—one focused on object-centric foreground information (from an Imagenet-pretrained model) and another on scene-centric background context (from a Places365-pretrained model)—can improve adversarial robustness. This approach is based on the idea that adversarial attacks may have divergent effects on these two feature spaces, and the unaffected stream can compensate to maintain classification performance without adversarial retraining.
related_work: Current deep learning networks are susceptible to adversarial attacks. Existing defense methods, like adversarial training, often do not generalize across different attack types and can compromise classification accuracy on clean data. While multimodal fusion has been explored for tasks like scene and action recognition by combining features from different sources (e.g., Places and Imagenet), its application as a defense mechanism against adversarial attacks is not well-established. This work proposes to use a biologically-inspired, multi-stream fusion approach to improve adversarial robustness as a complementary strategy to existing defenses.
abstract: Mammalian brains handle complex reasoning tasks by integrating information from specialized regions, which allows for improved robustness. In contrast, deep neural networks typically process a single information stream and are susceptible to adversarial perturbations. While many defense methods exist, they often do not generalize and can negatively affect performance on clean data. We propose a fusion model using a combination of background and foreground features extracted in parallel from Places-CNN and Imagenet-CNN. We plan to test the benefits of this fusion approach on preserving adversarial robustness for both human-perceivable (e.g., Gaussian blur) and network-perceivable (e.g., gradient-based) attacks. We propose that this biologically inspired approach provides a new way to improve adversarial robustness that can be complementary to current state-of-the-art approaches.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the contextual fusion model against human-perceivable perturbations, specifically Gaussian blur applied only to the foreground object.
experiments.Method: The model consists of a foreground classifier (ResNet18 pretrained on Imagenet), a background classifier (ResNet18 pretrained on Places365), and a joint classifier that fuses features from both using late fusion. The experiment will be run on a pruned version of the MS COCO dataset. Gaussian blur of varying intensity (sigma) will be applied to the foreground object in the test images. The performance of the three classifiers will be compared. The experiment will also be conducted on subsets of MS COCO with 'Similar' and 'Dissimilar' contexts to test the effect of context variability.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of the blur intensity (sigma)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the contextual fusion model as a defense against network-perceivable, gradient-based adversarial attacks (FGSM).
experiments.Method: Adversarial examples will be generated using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) with varying attack strengths (epsilon), crafted specifically to attack the foreground classifier. The performance of the foreground, background, and joint classifiers will be evaluated on these adversarial examples. This experiment will be conducted on both a pruned MS COCO dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of the attack strength (epsilon)']

experiments.Description: To investigate if regularizing the targeted (foreground) stream in the joint model can further enhance performance when the nature of the adversarial attack is known.
experiments.Method: An L2 penalty, controlled by a hyperparameter alpha, will be added to the loss function to regularize the weights of the foreground network within the joint classifier. This is intended to bias the joint classifier's decision towards the more reliable background stream. The performance of this regularized joint network will be evaluated on FGSM-attacked images from the MS COCO dataset and compared against a standard adversarial retraining defense baseline (Madry et al., 2017).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Classification accuracy as a function of attack strength (epsilon) for different levels of regularization (alpha)']

risk_factors: The degree of success for this method partially depends on the amount of variability in the background data.
In this work, we used a simple method of fusion - a single fully connected layer integrating the outputs of the object-centric and scene-centric networks.

title: DISTRIBUTED MULTI-AGENT DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
name: DG-MAPPO
short_hypothesis: A fully distributed MARL framework, DG-MAPPO, which leverages a novel Distributed Graph Attention Network (D-GAT) for multi-hop communication, can learn effective cooperative policies using only local observations, peer-to-peer communication, and shared/averaged rewards, thereby removing the need for centralized critics or privileged global information during training.
related_work: The dominant paradigm in MARL is Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE), used by methods like MAPPO and MADDPG. However, CTDE relies on privileged global state information during training, which creates scalability bottlenecks and a train-test mismatch. While some fully distributed learning techniques exist, they often retain some form of centralization, such as simple value function averaging or shared network parameters. Similarly, GNN-augmented MARL approaches have improved communication but still depend on centralized critics or value decomposition. This leaves a research gap for a MARL framework where agents can learn cooperative behaviors using only local observations and peer-to-peer communication, without any reliance on centralized components.
abstract: Centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) has been the dominant paradigm in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), but its reliance on global state information during training introduces scalability, robustness, and generalization bottlenecks. In practical scenarios, distributed approaches allow agents to adapt using only local information and peer-to-peer communication. We present a distributed MARL framework that removes the need for centralized critics or global information. Firstly, we develop a novel Distributed Graph Attention Network (D-GAT) that performs global state inference through multi-hop communication, where agents integrate neighbor features via input-dependent attention weights in a fully distributed manner. Leveraging D-GAT, we develop the distributed graph-attention MAPPO (DG-MAPPO) – a distributed MARL framework where agents optimize local policies and value functions using local observations, multi-hop communication, and shared/averaged rewards. This distributed MARL framework aims to provide a principled and scalable solution for robust collaboration, eliminating the need for centralized training or global observability, enabling agents to learn and act solely through peer-to-peer communication.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of DG-MAPPO on a suite of cooperative multi-agent tasks against strong CTDE baselines under both default and sparse communication settings.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the StarCraftII Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) benchmark. Tasks include: 3m, 8m, MMM, 5m vs 6m, 8m vs 9m, 10m vs 11m, 25m, MMM2, 6h vs 8z, and 3s5z vs 3s6z. The model will be compared against strong CTDE baselines: MAPPO, HAPPO, and MAT-Dec. An additional experiment will assess performance in highly sparse settings by reducing the communication range on the 'Hard+' scenarios (6h vs 8z and MMM2).

experiments.Description: To evaluate DG-MAPPO's performance in a complex, dynamic environment with a changing communication topology.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the Google Research Football (GFootball) benchmark, specifically in the 'Academy 3 vs 1 with Keeper' scenario. It will be compared against CTDE baselines including HAPPO and MAT-Dec.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DG-MAPPO's robustness in continuous-control settings with a fixed, sparse communication topology where agents can only exchange information with physically adjacent joints.
experiments.Method: The proposed DG-MAPPO model will be evaluated on the Multi-Agent MuJoCo benchmark, specifically the 'Multi-HalfCheetah (6x1)' task. It will be compared against CTDE baselines including HAPPO and MAT-Dec.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to assess the importance of individual components of the DG-MAPPO framework: the attention mechanism for message aggregation, the number of communication hops, and the consensus loss regularizer.
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on selected 'Hard' and 'Hard+' SMAC environments (10m vs 11m, 6h vs 8z, and MMM2). The experiments will involve comparing variants of DG-MAPPO: (1) using mean-based aggregation versus attention-augmented aggregation, (2) varying the number of communication hops, and (3) training with and without the consensus loss.

risk_factors: DG-MAPPO, by design, uses the same communication mechanism during both training and execution. At inference time, agents continue to exchange feature vectors h^i with neighbors via the D-GAT module. While this incurs a non-zero communication overhead during deployment, the execution-time communication cost remains bounded and tunable by design.

title: Optimizing Q-Learning Using Expectile Regression: A Dual Approach to Handle In-Sample and Out-of-Sample Data
name: EIQL-expectile-regression-offline-rl
short_hypothesis: A novel offline RL algorithm, Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL), can improve performance by stochastically combining in-sample updates from Implicit Q-Learning with out-of-sample Bellman updates. This dual approach is intended to leverage the maximization capability of the Bellman update for potentially valuable out-of-sample actions while mitigating error extrapolation risks by controlling the frequency of such updates.
related_work: Current offline RL methods face a distribution shift problem. Some approaches penalize out-of-distribution (OOD) actions to ensure pessimism, but balancing this to avoid suboptimal solutions is difficult. Other methods, like Implicit Q-Learning (IQL), use in-sample learning with expectile regression to avoid querying unseen actions altogether. This work addresses the gap of how to safely incorporate potentially beneficial OOD actions, which IQL avoids, without suffering from the error extrapolation that plagues standard Q-learning.
abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) presents unique challenges, primarily due to the constraint of learning from a static dataset without additional environmental interaction. Traditional methods often face limitations in effectively exploiting the available data, particularly when navigating the exploration-exploitation trade-off inherent in RL. This paper introduces a novel algorithm inspired by Implicit Q-Learning, designed to extend the utility of the Bellman update to actions not explicitly present in the dataset. Our approach, termed Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL), strategically incorporates actions beyond the dataset constraints by allowing selection actions with maximum Q. By doing so, it leverages the maximization capability of the Bellman update, while simultaneously mitigating error extrapolation risks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Extended Implicit Q-Learning (EIQL) algorithm against prior offline RL methods across a wide range of domains and dataset compositions from the D4RL benchmark.
experiments.Method: EIQL will be tested on the D4RL benchmark. This includes Gym-MuJoCo locomotion environments (halfcheetah, hopper, walker2d) on random, medium, medium-replay, medium-expert, and expert datasets; Adroit tasks (pen-human-v0, pen-cloned-v0); Franka kitchen tasks (kitchen-complete-v0, kitchen-partial-v0, kitchen-mixed-v0); and AntMaze tasks (umaze, medium-play, medium-diverse, large-play, large-diverse). The performance will be compared against baselines including Behavioral Cloning (BC), TD3+BC, Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), and Implicit Q-Learning (IQL).

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of using different expectile levels in the value function update on policy performance and learning dynamics, particularly in scenarios with different reward characteristics.
experiments.Method: The EIQL algorithm will be run with various expectile levels on three distinct datasets: `walker2d-medium-replay-v2`, `antmaze-large-play-v2`, and `kitchen-partial-v0`. The learning dynamics will be compared across these settings and against the baseline IQL.

risk_factors: In future work, we plan to explore the potential of our method to improve other in-sample learning techniques, with the aim of assessing the generalizability of our approach across different domains and settings.

title: Towards Second-Order Optimization in Learned Image Compression: Faster, Better, and More Deployable
name: soap-for-lic
short_hypothesis: Replacing standard first-order optimizers like Adam with a second-order quasi-Newton optimizer, SOAP, will act as a seamless drop-in replacement that dramatically improves training efficiency and final rate-distortion performance for Learned Image Compression (LIC) models. This is because SOAP's Newton preconditioning will inherently resolve the intra-step and inter-step gradient conflicts that are intrinsic to the rate-distortion objective, leading to faster, more stable convergence and producing models with fewer outliers, which enhances deployability.
related_work: Training Learned Image Compression (LIC) models typically uses first-order optimizers like Adam, but this approach suffers from slow convergence and suboptimal performance due to gradient conflicts between the rate and distortion objectives. Previous work has tried to accelerate training via methods like auxiliary transforms (AuxT), parameter decomposition (CMD-LIC), or explicit gradient re-weighting (Balanced R-D). However, these strategies often introduce additional architectural complexity, rely on fragile hyperparameter tuning, or require non-trivial modifications to the training pipeline. This creates a need for a method that can accelerate training and mitigate gradient conflicts without these added overheads, ideally as a simple drop-in replacement for the standard optimizer.
abstract: Training learned image compression (LIC) models entails navigating a challenging optimization landscape defined by the fundamental trade-off between rate and distortion. Standard first-order optimizers, such as SGD and Adam, struggle with gradient conflicts arising from competing objectives, leading to slow convergence and suboptimal rate–distortion performance. In this work, we propose that a simple switch to a second-order quasi-Newton optimizer, SOAP, will dramatically improve both training efficiency and final performance across diverse LIC architectures. Our theoretical and empirical analyses are designed to reveal that SOAP’s Newton preconditioning inherently resolves the intra-step and inter-step update conflicts intrinsic to the R–D objective, facilitating faster, more stable convergence. Beyond acceleration, we aim to uncover a critical deployability benefit: that SOAP-trained models will exhibit significantly fewer activation and latent outliers, which would improve entropy modeling and substantially enhance robustness to post-training quantization.

experiments.Description: To empirically demonstrate the benefits of second-order optimization (SOAP) on training acceleration and final rate-distortion (R-D) performance compared to a first-order method (Adam) and other specialized training strategies across several representative LIC architectures.
experiments.Method:
Models: ELIC, TCM, LALIC, and DCAE.
Training Data: COCO 2017 dataset.
Evaluation Datasets: Kodak, Tecnick, and CLIC 2022.
Baselines: The primary baseline is the Adam optimizer. Comparisons will also be made against other training strategies including AuxT, CMD-LIC, and Balanced-RD.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Steps-to-Adam: The ratio of training steps required to reach a target validation loss, normalized by the number required by Adam.', 'Time-to-Adam: The ratio of training time to reach a target validation loss, relative to Adam.', 'BD-Rate after Convergence: The average bitrate savings at matched image quality between a model and its Adam-trained counterpart after full convergence.']

experiments.Description: To validate the theoretical hypothesis that SOAP's performance gains stem from its ability to mitigate gradient conflicts by aligning update vectors both within a single step (intra-step) and across consecutive steps (inter-step).
experiments.Method: The ELIC model will be trained with both Adam and SOAP, initialized from a pretrained model to observe behavior near a local minimum. The cosine similarity of update vectors will be tracked during training.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intra-step score (S_intra): Cosine similarity between the rate and distortion update vectors within a single step.', 'Inter-step score (S_inter): Cosine similarity of the total update vector across consecutive steps.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that SOAP-trained models produce fewer latent and activation outliers, and to evaluate whether this translates to enhanced robustness under post-training quantization (PTQ).
experiments.Method: Models (ELIC, TCM, LALIC, DCAE) trained with Adam and SOAP will be evaluated. Outlier statistics will be measured on the Kodak dataset. PTQ robustness will be assessed using W8A8 (int8 weights and activations) quantization with the AdaRound method. Latent scaled deviation maps for the ELIC model will also be visualized.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Kurt(X): A measure of the tailedness of channel energies.', 'MaxMed(X): A measure of per-sample extreme values relative to typical magnitudes.', 'ΔBD-Rate (%): The change in BD-Rate after applying W8A8 post-training quantization, to measure robustness.']

risk_factors: The argument is layerwise and ignores inter-layer curvature; nonetheless, in practice, the rotated-space diagonalization substantially improves conditioning compared to first-order methods.
Finite-sample EMAs, infrequent preconditioner updates, and regularization (+δI) introduce additional approximation error.

The Adam preconditioner tracks (diagonal) second moments of gradients (Fisher-like), not the exact Hessian diagonal; the identification in equation 15 is a local approximation strongest when diag(HGN) ≈ diag(H) near the optimum.
